issue_title: Muffin handlers hang when using a Rethinkdb connecton that was made outside of Muffin's loop
issue_body:
For some reason Muffin handlers hang when using a Rethinkdb connecton that was made outside of Muffin's loop. I realise that it isn't necessarily Muffin's fault, but please take a look at the very simple examples below.

**The following script works as expected** (returns "1+1 = 2" on each page load):

```python
import muffin
import rethinkdb as r

r.set_loop_type('asyncio')

app = muffin.Application('example')


@app.register('/')
async def handle(request):
	db = await r.connect()  # <--- Connect every time
	js = '1+1'
	res = await r.js(js).run(db)
	text = "%s = %s" % (js, res)
	return text


if __name__ == '__main__':
	app.manage()
```

**The following script hangs on page load** (and then it dies in long agony on ^C rather than simply quits):

```python
import muffin
import asyncio
import rethinkdb as r

r.set_loop_type('asyncio')

db = asyncio.get_event_loop().run_until_complete(r.connect())  # <--- Connect only once

app = muffin.Application('example')


@app.register('/')
async def handle(request):
	js = '1+1'
	res = await r.js(js).run(db)
	text = "%s = %s" % (js, res)
	return text


if __name__ == '__main__':
	app.manage()
```

I don't think it's purely Rethinkdb problem because **the same approach works with `aiohttp`**:

```python
from aiohttp import web
import asyncio
import rethinkdb as r

r.set_loop_type('asyncio')

db = asyncio.get_event_loop().run_until_complete(r.connect())  # <--- Connect only once


async def handle(request):
	js = '1+1'
	res = await r.js(js).run(db)
	text = "%s = %s" % (js, res)
	return web.Response(body=text.encode('utf-8'))


app = web.Application()
app.router.add_route('GET', '/', handle)

web.run_app(app)
```

issue_title: New error when mixing gl and gles on web service
issue_body:
I am working with an open source project called [lovr](https://github.com/bjornbytes/lovr) which [uses glad](https://github.com/bjornbytes/lovr/tree/master/src/lib/glad). Its current GLAD generation link is [this URL](http://glad.dav1d.de/#profile=core&language=c&specification=gl&api=gl%3D3.3&api=gles2%3D3.0&extensions=GL_ARB_texture_storage&extensions=GL_EXT_texture_compression_s3tc&extensions=GL_EXT_texture_filter_anisotropic&extensions=GL_EXT_texture_sRGB&extensions=GL_OVR_multiview
).

Sometime in the last week or three, this URL stopped working. Attempting to generate a GLAD from this URL on the web service now results in a message

> Warnings/Errors:
> Core profile not available when selecting any gles API, automatically changed

It then generates a Compatibility profile.

When I spoke to the lovr developer, he explained that the goal was to get a Core profile when running with plain GL and whatever profile when running with gles2. This is because the code targets both desktops and on emscripten, and Desktop-OpenGL Core is more similar to gles2 than OpenGL Compatibility is.

There are two issues here:
- What would be the correct way for lovr to get its gl core + gles2-supporting headers? I don't know what it was the web service was doing before when it simply allowed "gles2 core", but it *worked*.
- *Even if what Lovr is doing is incorrect*, it is problematic for there to be URLs which previously worked on the web service but now no longer work. I maintain a fork of Lovr where I use additional extensions that Lovr master does not use. Previously I could add or remove extensions by loading Lovr's URL up and changing the extensions list to add or remove one or two extensions. However now running the URL produces a compatibility profile, which is not what Lovr targets. In other words, removing support for core+gles outputs means third party consumers of older GLAD-using projects are "stuck"— I cannot necessarily patch Lovr to migrate it away from "core+gles2" because I do not directly control the original project, but I can't generate a new core+gles2 source file either.

I am not certain what my "expected behavior" here is, but: Would it be possible to alter the web service so that generating the "illegal" core+gles2 profile either becomes a *warning* (ie emits the illegal profile rather than silently altering it to compatibility) or provides more useful advice about what to do?

Thanks

issue_title: Potential memory leak.
issue_body: There's a potential memory leak in get_exts.  realloc can return null.  If it does exts_i will be null and you'll have leaked the memory exts_i was previously pointing to (realloc doesn't free the previous block if the new block couldn't be allocated).  Then you'll deference it in the proceeding loop.

issue_title: Unit does not support `__ne__`
issue_body:
`Unit` defines `__eq__`: https://github.com/hgrecco/pint/blob/004610b3ff78155d4660f1ac94002bd5509717ff/pint/unit.py#L197

`Unit` also defines `__lt__`, `__le__`, `__ge__`, `__gt__`: https://github.com/hgrecco/pint/blob/004610b3ff78155d4660f1ac94002bd5509717ff/pint/unit.py#L222

Unit does not appear to define `__ne__`.

```
>>> import pint
>>> r = pint.UnitRegistry()
>>> 
>>> # Compare same instance.
... b = r.Unit('byte')
>>> b == b
True
>>> b != b
False
>>> 
>>> # Compare different instances.
... r.Unit('byte') == r.Unit('byte')
True
>>> r.Unit('byte') != r.Unit('byte')
True
```

I didn't check other classes.

issue_title: Investigate for Memory Leaks
issue_body:
While reading [this](https://reproducingnetworkresearch.wordpress.com/2016/05/30/cs244-16-an-accurate-sampling-scheme-for-detecting-syn-flooding-attacks-and-portscans/) article, I noticed that the author talks about a memory leak in dpkt. It seems that they were trying to reproduce [this](http://mkorczynski.com/An%20Accurate%20Sampling%20Scheme%20for%20Detecting%20SYN.pdf) paper's results.

> Unfortunately, after writing most of our code and testing on smaller datasets, we ran it on a larger file and discovered a memory leak in the library. This made it tricky to run our code on very large pcap files, and ended up capping the max number of packets we could process  and analyze to 27 million (on a machine running with 32 GB of RAM). 

They go on to say the following in the feedback section of the paper:

> The main issue was with the memory leak in dpkt. We were able to get around it by just running our code on machines with a lot of memory (Felipe’s laptop has 16GB of RAM, and Keziah spun up an AWS instance with 32 GB of RAM and we stopped having problems). However, if you want to run locally on machines with less memory, I would avoid this library.

Specifically about this issue - I dont yet know where/what leak they found, but I am going to ping them about it. Hopefully we can reproduce & fix this, iff this is a problem with dpkt.

It is easier to blame this on python's internal memory allocator, which does not release memory back to the OS, but lets see. Maybe we can add some code patterns to the examples which could be used as a workaround (for not just dpkt, but for python performance/memory problems in general).  

Update: 
- The dataset is available [here](http://www.caida.org/data/passive/ddos-20070804_dataset.xml)
- The code used in the test is available [here](https://github.com/felipems/cs244_final_project)

issue_title: Add bounds checking to IPv6 extension header parsing loop
issue_body:
_From [aa...@monkey.org](https://code.google.com/u/116105672249653840815/) on April 25, 2011 09:12:48_

Currently there is no bounds checking and a traceback will occur if the packet data is shorter than the purported extension header option length.

FWIW, I'm not using this exact diff in my project.  Instead of raising dpkt.UnpackError, I am just silently breaking out of the loop.  That way most of the pertinent IPv6 data (up and to including the bad extension header) is successfully parsed out of the packet and accessible, as opposed to having only the Ethernet portion.  That said, I felt raising the error fit better with the existing code.  If the maintainers disagree then feel free to change this.

**Attachment:** [dpkt-ipv6-bounds-check.diff](http://code.google.com/p/dpkt/issues/detail?id=72)

issue_title: no_command rule doesn't know about dynamically defined aliases/functions
issue_body:
If you define an alias `foo` on the command line by doing
```
alias foo="echo foo"
```
then this won't be a suggestion for a typo like `fooo`.  Similarly for a function
```
function foo { echo foo; }
```
This is counter intuitive when the following works just fine (assuming `$HOME/bin` is in your `$PATH`)
```
echo echo foo > $HOME/bin/foo
chmod +x $HOME/bin/foo
```

Fixing this is basically impossible with thefuck's current design (which is "spawn a new shell and see what alias/functions it has").  Supporting this use case would require substantial help from the alias to be able to know these things about the current shell environment, and then possibly also assistance for actually running the alias/function.

See also https://github.com/nvbn/thefuck/issues/209#issuecomment-104093437.

issue_title: Support using same file path for input & output
issue_body:
Sox doesn't support using the same file as both input and output - doing this will result in an empty, invalid audio file. While this is sox behavior and not pysox, it would be nice if pysox took care of this behind the scenes. Right now the user needs to worry about this logic themselves, e.g. like this:

```python
import tempfile
import shutil
from scaper.util import _close_temp_files

audio_infile = '/Users/justin/Downloads/trimtest.wav'
audio_outfile = '/Users/justin/Downloads/trimtest.wav'
start_time = 2
end_time = 3

tfm = sox.Transformer()
tfm.trim(start_time, end_time)
if audio_outfile != audio_infile:
    tfm.build(audio_infile, audio_outfile)
else:
    # must use temp file in order to save to same file
    tmpfiles = []
    with _close_temp_files(tmpfiles):
        # Create tmp file
        tmpfiles.append(
            tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(
                suffix='.wav', delete=True))
        # Save trimmed result to temp file
        tfm.build(audio_infile, tmpfiles[-1].name)
        # Copy result back to original file
        shutil.copyfile(tmpfiles[-1].name, audio_outfile)
```

Pysox *does* issue a warning when a file is about to be overwritten, which is even more confusing under this scenario since the user (who might be unfamiliar with the quirks of sox) has no reason to think that the overwritten file will be invalid.

issue_title: Automatically refresh static assets/resources

When the assets are updated (often during development, for the user upon a new release) the browser still has them cached and a "forced" refresh is needed. issue_title: dump() parameters are switched compared to json, pickle
issue_body:
pytoml looks like it's mirroring the API of modules like json and pickle, with `load[s]` and `dump[s]` methods. But whereas json and pickle have `dump(obj,  fd)`, pytoml has `dump(fd, obj)`.

Obviously fixing this would be a breaking API change, so maybe it's too late. But it's a minor annoyance.

Any chance this capability can be added to plop?  (crossposted as vmprof/vmprof-python#54)

issue_title: Support for derived exceptions
issue_body:
Hi, 
I've been using `six` on a daily basis in all of my Python projects, so first of all, thanks !

I have an enhancement proposal: it would be quite useful to be able to use `six` to differentiate builtin exceptions depending on the Python version: instead of using always the general exception to make sure it is compatible with any Python version, or to define an alias at the beginning of the program, six would make it possible to write:
```python
raise six.errors.FileNotFoundError("...")
```
to raise a `FileNotFoundError` on Python 3.3 and later, or an `OSError` on earlier versions.


Interesting candidates would be:
* `OSError` subclasses added in Python 3.3: `IsADirectoryError`, `PermissionError`, etc.
* `ModuleNotFoundError` added in Python 3.6
* `RecursionError`, added in Python 3.5

I think this would ease the forward compatibility of the code, allowing to raise more specific errors if available in the current Python version.

issue_title: Unregistered Exception does not provide timestamp field
issue_body:
Referring to apple's spec here:

https://developer.apple.com/library/content/documentation/NetworkingInternet/Conceptual/RemoteNotificationsPG/CommunicatingwithAPNs.html#//apple_ref/doc/uid/TP40008194-CH11-SW16


> If the value in the :status header is 410, the value of this key is the last time at which APNs confirmed that the device token was no longer valid for the topic.
> 
> Stop pushing notifications until the device registers a token with a later timestamp with your provider.
> 

If you add the timestamp value to the exception then it would be possible to check the timestamp of when the push token was last provided by the client and whether or not it should in fact be removed.

issue_title: Proven RFC-compliant datetime parser
issue_body:
I'm too lazy for making PR. This code works for Python >= 3.2 (thanks to datetime.timezone).
This code is faster and also much more RFC compliant.

```python
import datetime
import re

qwe = re.compile(
    r'''
    ^
    (?P<Year>[0-9]{4})
    (?P<Month>0[1-9]|1[0-2])
    (?P<Day>0[1-9]|[12][0-9]|3[01])
    (?P<Hour>[01][0-9]|2[0-3])
    (?:
      (?P<Minute>[0-5][0-9])
      (?P<Second>[0-5][0-9]|60)?
    )?
    (?:
      [.,]
      (?P<Fraction>[0-9]+)
    )?  
    (?:
      Z
      |
      (?:
        (?P<Offset>[+-])
        (?P<OffHour>[01][0-9]|2[0-3])
        (?P<OffMinute>[0-5][0-9])?
      )
    )
    $
    ''',
    re.VERBOSE
)


def parse(string: str) -> datetime.datetime:
    match = qwe.fullmatch(string)
    if match is None:
        raise ValueError
    matches = match.groupdict()

    offset = datetime.timedelta(
        hours=int(matches['OffHour'] or 0),
        minutes=int(matches['OffMinute'] or 0)
    )

    if matches['Offset'] == '-':
        offset *= -1

    # Python does not support leap second in datetime (!)
    if matches['Second'] == '60':
        matches['Second'] = '59'

    # According to RFC, fraction may be applied to an Hour/Minute (!)
    fraction = float('0.' + (matches['Fraction'] or '0'))

    if matches['Minute'] is None:
        fraction *= 60
        minute = int(fraction)
        fraction -= minute
    else:
        minute = int(matches['Minute'])

    if matches['Second'] is None:
        fraction *= 60
        second = int(fraction)
        fraction -= second
    else:
        second = int(matches['Second'])

    microseconds = int(fraction * 1000000)

    return datetime.datetime(
        int(matches['Year']),
        int(matches['Month']),
        int(matches['Day']),
        int(matches['Hour']),
        minute,
        second,
        microseconds,
        datetime.timezone(offset),
    )


# 30:30.42 = 30*60 + 30.42 = 1830.42 / 3600 ~= 0.50845
print(parse('2018020215.50845Z'))
assert parse('199412161032Z') == parse('199412160532-0500')  # test from RFC
```

I'm using Ubuntu 12.04 and the screenshot shows how I have two different entries, shown by seahorse 3.2.2 with different "formats" (one has icon, the other one doesn't, etc.)

My application code, the one that uses keyring is:

```python
def get_secret(host, user)
    secret = keyring.get_password(host, user)
    
    if secret is None:
        chars = string.ascii_uppercase + string.digits + string.lowercase
        secret = ''.join(random.choice(chars) for _ in range(24))
        fmt = 'Randomly generated secret for %s@%s: %s'

```

My code doesn't use threads; and something I've seen is that the two entries in the screenshot have different passwords. issue_title: Need timezone-aware command in scheduler
issue_body:
tempora provides the `scheduler.PeriodicCommandFixedDelay.daily_at`, which will create an event at a point in time (timezone-aware), and then schedule follow up events at a fixed delay of 1 day after each. But across a DST boundary, 9am one day may be 10am 24 hours later (such as happened in the US this past Sunday).

The scheduler should offer an option to execute the command at a particular time of day, honoring timezone effects.

issue_title: SPF support could be confusing
issue_body:
I note that the SPF RR type was discontinued in RFC 7208 (see https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7208#section-3.1). I tried searching for my own SPF records with the SPF type, but of course they were not found (they do show up as TXT records, which they are). This could lead to confusion as to whether the command should return SPF records (which are implemented as standard TXT records) or records of the SPF RR type (which has been discontinued).

For clarity, I would suggest that lexicon drop the SPF RR type. Alternately, you could allow listing the SPF RR type (possibly with a printed message for the user that notes that SPF records implemented as TXT records will not be returned), and disallow creation of SPF RRs (since current RFC specifies that  it must be implemented by TXT record only).

issue_title: _check_tunnel fails when using adresses other than 127.0.0.1
issue_body:
Hello, 

First of all thank you for this great library !!

In `_check_tunnel(self, _srv)`, to avoid Windows error when trying to connect to `0.0.0.0`, use of `127.0.0.1` is forced:

```python
# Windows raises WinError 10049 if trying to connect to `0.0.0.0`
s.connect(('127.0.0.1', _srv.local_port))
```
When setting up tunnels on addresses picked from `127.0.0.1/8` (loopback ip addres range) (e.g. `127.1.1.1`), `_check_tunnel(self, _srv)` still uses `127.0.0.1` instead of `127.1.1.1` which gives wrong result (a `DOWN` status instead of an `UP`)

Following code would allow to correctly check those tunnels:

```python
# Windows raises WinError 10049 if trying to connect to 0.0.0.0
addr = '127.0.0.1' if _srv.local_address == '0.0.0.0' else _srv.local_host
s.connect((addr, _srv.local_port))
```

Regards

issue_title: Generating UUID in freeze_time context
issue_body:
There seems to be an issue with generating UUIDs when the freeze time is set to a future time. For some reason, all UUIDs generated outside the freeze context will be associated with the frozen timestamp.  However, the context seems to be released properly when the freeze time is set to the past. 
Any ideas?

Here are some tests to illustrate (The second unit test will also fail if both tests are run in sequence.):

```python
import uuid
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from cassandra.util import datetime_from_uuid1
from freezegun import freeze_time

# Fails. uuid1() will generate the same timestamp uuid after the freeze context.
# assert datetime.datetime(2016, 9, 15, 13, 27, 48, 527016) > 
#   datetime.datetime(2016, 9, 15, 13, 27, 48, 527016)
def test_uuid_with_future_freeze_time():
    with freeze_time(datetime.utcnow() + timedelta(hours=2)):
        uuid1 = uuid.uuid1()

    uuid2 = uuid.uuid1()
    assert datetime_from_uuid1(uuid1) > datetime_from_uuid1(uuid2)


# Passes
def test_uuid_with_past_freeze_time():
    with freeze_time(datetime.utcnow() - timedelta(hours=2)):
        uuid1 = uuid.uuid1()

    uuid2 = uuid.uuid1()
    assert datetime_from_uuid1(uuid1) < datetime_from_uuid1(uuid2)
```

issue_title: Kafka Producer in a daemon process it not works.
Only use os- and processor- specific enum values on the appropriate os and processor
issue_body:
So this is the _actual_ cause that was behind yesterday's issue, though that is probably still a problem.

If you look at how this project tries to load the file linked from this issue https://github.com/angr/cle/issues/103#issuecomment-355405436 it crashes because it thinks the dynamic tag 0x6000000f is `DT_SUNW_FILTER`, and assumes that the corresponding d_val points to a string. However, this is not solaris, and 0x6000000f is not a filter here, so the pointer is not an index into a string table, and so the lookup crashes.

This seems like a very complicated and invasive change, and I don't know anything about the architecture of this project, so I'd appreciate a response other than "patches welcome".

issue_title: Additional special characters
issue_body:
Hi, we've been working on integrating `scanf` into one of our projects ([here](https://github.com/DMSC-Instrument-Data/lewis/pull/227)) as an alternative for regular expressions (using `scanf_compile` to generate regexes). Many of the patterns we had defined previously were of the type `r'^\d+$'`, with `^` and `$` to make sure there's nothing else in the string.

When moving some patterns to `scanf`, we forgot to remove `^` and `$` and expected that those characters would be matched as literals. I tried to add those (and `|`) to the escaped characters in `scanf.py` and then it worked as (we) expected.

Before opening any pull request I thought I'd ask whether it was intended to work the way it's currently implemented, because with the changes it's more difficult to do the beginning and end of string matching (there would probably need to be some additional argument to add that to the generated regex).

Looking forward to your feedback! In the meantime we're going to implement a workaround on our end, because we really want to use `scanf` :)

make non-ascii characters rendered on the console.

issue_title: Auto Reconnect Error: Future exception was never retrieved
issue_body:
I am trying to setup a robust connection like this:
```
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import asyncio
import logging
from aio_pika import connect_robust

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG)

connection = None
channel = None


def handle_exception(loop, context):
    logging.error('exception in event loop: {}'.format(context['message']))
    try:
        logging.error('Future: {}'.format(context['future']))
    except KeyError:
        pass
    try:
        logging.error('Handle: {}'.format(context['handle']))
    except KeyError:
        pass
    try:
        ex = context['exception']
        logging.error('Exception: {} ({})'.format(ex, type(ex).__qualname__))
    except KeyError:
        pass


async def rmq_connect():
    global connection, channel
    connection = await connect_robust('amqp://guest:guest@127.0.0.1/')
    connection.add_reconnect_callback(lambda c: logging.info('Reconnecting to {}'.format(c)))
    channel = await connection.channel()


loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.set_exception_handler(handle_exception)
loop.run_until_complete(rmq_connect())

try:
    loop.run_forever()
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    logging.info('KeyboardInterrupt - closing connection')
finally:
    loop.run_until_complete(connection.close())

loop.close()

```

Basically this works. During a reconnect, there are some reported Exceptions such as

> ERROR:root:exception in event loop: Task exception was never retrieved
> ERROR:root:Future: <Task finished coro=<RobustConnection.connect() done, defined at aio_pika/robust_connection.py:109> exception=The AMQP connection was closed: ()>
> ERROR:root:Exception:  (ConnectionClosed)

and

> ERROR:root:exception in event loop: Future exception was never retrieved
> ERROR:root:Future: <Future finished exception=ConnectionError('Auto Reconnect Error',)>
> ERROR:root:Exception: Auto Reconnect Error (ConnectionError)

But if the connection resumes, and I stop the program, then at the very end I get a lot of those

> ERROR:root:exception in event loop: Future exception was never retrieved
> ERROR:root:Future: <Future finished exception=ConnectionError('Auto Reconnect Error',)>
> ERROR:root:Exception: Auto Reconnect Error (ConnectionError)

This makes me worry that there remains something unclear from the reconnection that could gather over time. Is there something to be done differently in the code with respect to error handling and cleanup?

issue_title: SHA-1 signature check is too strict
issue_body:
Hello,

The check for SHA-1-based signatures in a certificate chain is currently unnecessarily strict: it fails if the self-signed root certificate is signed using SHA-1.  The signature on a root certificate is irrelevant; root certificates are trusted based on their identity, not based on their signatures.  Even Google still uses a root certificate signed using SHA-1.  See https://www.entrust.com/need-sha-2-signed-root-certificates/ for some more details and commentary.

issue_title: Licensing clarification
issue_body:
sslyze lists it's license as GPLv2. Can you clarify if this is an intentional choice, or one made for historical reasons? 

GPLv2 makes perfect sense for the use of sslyze as a standalone tool, and preventing users from building a proprietary SSL analysis tool based off that codebase. 

However, I've got a use case that would involved deploying sslyze as part of a Software-as-a-Service platform. Under the terms of the GPLv2, this would be a completely legitimate usage of sslyze, and I would *not* be required to distribute the source code for my SaaS platform (see [this FAQ on the GNU website](https://www.gnu.org/licenses/gpl-faq.html#UnreleasedMods)).

While I'm completely covered under the *letter* of the law, I'd rather not violate the *spirit* of the law in the way the project intended the sslyze API and codebase to be used. 

Are you able to:
* Clarify that you have no objection to sslyze being used as part of a proprietary SaaS deployment; and/or
* Provide (either to me specifically, or more generally) a license with more liberal terms (LGPL would be fine; MIT/BSD would be even better); or
* Modify the license to be AGPL, or something that covers the SaaS use case?

issue_title: Improve stacktraces
issue_body:
Currently, when something goes wrong inside of one of the custom validators, it can be really nasty to trace down exactly what is happening.  This is largely due to most validation occuring in curried (`functools.partial(...)`) calls to validation functions which makes tracing down exactly *who* is passing the wrong type into something frustrating.

Some ideas to fix this are...

- modify the return value of `flex.validation.common.generate_object_validator` to have a more descriptive name.
- modify `flex.functional.chain_reduce` and `flex.functional.apply_functions_to_key` to have more descriptive names.

issue_title: License file and release notes are confusing and non-intuitive to find.
issue_body:
Storing the license file as an oddly named file in src/pypubsub is very much not discoverable, and seems to be done for no reason other than to store it as package_data even though no one who was completely unable to discover either that or the release notes in the standard locations in the github web interface, is likely to go digging around in the egg directory.

On which note it looks like zip_safe=False may be being set just to make these files available outside of a zip, even though they're not relevant to the functionality of python at all. In fact, most people would just look at the setuptools metata via e.g. `pip show pypubsub`, and if they need the license for legal reasons they don't need it **in the egg distribution** so it would make more sense to have it in the root of the repository.

I've literally never seen it done this way before. :(

Also, moving it to the root of the repository and giving it a standard name means github can automatically detect and display the status in the header bar for the repo. :)

  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "<stdin>", line 3, in bar
AttributeError: 'Foo' object has no attribute '_barr'
```
This is the expected behaviour, the bad call to `self._barr()` raises an `AttributeError` appropriately and tells us its from line 3 in the `bar` property implementation.

However when something causes an `AttributeError` beneath a `Dataset` property:
```python
>>> class Foo(Dataset):
...    @property
...    def bar(self): return self._barr()
...    
...    def _bar(self): return 'OK'
...
>>> foo = Foo()
>>> foo.bar
Traceback (most recent call last):
   File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
   File "...", line 480, in __getattr__
     if tag is None
AttributeError: Dataset does not have attribute 'bar'.
```
Which is not the expected behaviour and makes debugging harder as there's no indication what caused the original exception, instead everything is blamed on a missing attribute that clearly exists.

Occurs due to the `__getattr__` override.

issue_title: pyperclip exposes pygtk bug in Python 2 on Linux
issue_body:
There is is a bug in ``pygtk`` in Python 2 (but not Python 3) on Linux exposed by ``pyperclip``'s dependency on ``pygtk``.  This will prevent all output to **stdout** from background threads in an application which imports ``pyperclip`` and calls raw_input() to get input from **stdin**. It only occurs when all of the following conditions are true:

OS = Linux
Python version = 2.7
pygtk is installed

An extremely simple workaround is to add code like the following to ``pyperclip``:

```Python
# Workaround for pyperclip importing gtk and having it interfere with printing from raw_input()
try:
    import gtk
    gtk.set_interactive(0)
except ImportError:
    pass
```

issue_title: jsobj, slimit, ply. Are they truly needed?
issue_body:
As far as I know **slimit** and **ply** are used by **jsobj.py** just for grabbing metadata from the JavaScript on the page, the only reason I can see this being a good idea is if it is generated dynamically. If it isn't generated dynamically and just happens to be contained in the variables within the JavaScript then there is no reason it can't be retrieved with **BeautifulSoup** and **re**.

Especially since we get errors (albeit harmless) that throw users off and make them think something is broken because of slimit. (and it adds overhead)

I have tested and proven that we can get the same data with:
```
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup
import requests
import re
r = requests.get("http://guiguisuisui.bandcamp.com/album/wu-xing")
soup = BeautifulSoup(r.text, 'html.parser')
data = soup.find('div', id='pgBd').find_all("script", {"src":False})[1].string
embedStringBlock = re.search(r"(?<=var\sEmbedData\s=\s)[^;]*", data)
stringBlock = re.search(r"(?<=var\sTralbumData\s=\s)[^;]*", data).group()
```

issue_title: Refactor: move execution logic out of resttest.py to allow for xUnit Output Callbacks
issue_body:
Per https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/issues/163 this is in support of the xUnit changes

We are moving the test/benchmark execution logic into the macros themself:

[x] Move config/response methods and parser methods into parsing.py or a new macros.py class, and tests
[ ] Move test execution logic into an execute method within the test, and invoke that for running
[ ] Move benchmark execution logic into a benchmark execution method, and invoke that for running
[ ] Define callbacks and refactor the above to execute them  --> [hacky option to output JUnit here](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/junit-xml)
[ ] Move benchmark analysis methods into benchmark themself
[ ] Better name for the above classes (note, original names need to import content to allow for extensions to use it
[ ] Optional: Class based registry for macros, where it does not just register parse functions (prototype for v2?) - this allows for API-level use (beta for use in the other extension points, where they look to see if it's a parse function or a class input to figure out how to use them)

Macro Execution Signature: 
```python
execute_macro(self, context=None, testConfig=None, cmdlineArgs=None, callbacks=DefaultLogging, *args, *kwargs)
```
Lets you later first look for variables in self, then testConfig, then cmdlineArgs (which are a dictionary).

issue_title: Issue with file sometimes unable to be opened by Numbers
issue_body:
Hi,

I am using XlsxWriter to do export from Django through an HttpResponse and sometimes the file can't be opened by Numbers, but can always be opened by LibreOffice.

I am using Python version 2.7.12 and XlsxWriter 1.0.2 and Numbers version 4.3.1 5249 and LibreOffice version 5.3.4.2.

Here is basically how the response is coded, mashed into one script obviously:

```python

from django.http import HttpResponse
from django.views.generic.base import TemplateView
import xlsxwriter

class XlsxResponse(HttpResponse):
    def __init__(self, writer_klass, *args, **kwargs):
        kwargs["content_type"] = "application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.spreadsheetml.sheet"
        super(XlsxResponse, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        self._writer_klass = writer_klass
        self["Content-Disposition"] = "attachment; filename=\"export.xlsx\""

    def process(self, content):
        self._writer_klass(self).handle(content)


class XlsxWriter(object):
    def __init__(self, response):
        self._response = response
        self.wb = xlsxwriter.Workbook(response, {"in_memory": True})

    # This is implemented by the sub-classes, which handle worksheets
    # and formats and stuff.
    def _handle(self, content):
        raise NotImplementedError

    def handle(self, content):
        try:
            self._handle(content)
        finally:
            self.wb.close()


class CalendarExportView(TemplateView):
    def render_to_response(self, context, **response_kwargs):
        response = nexus_http.XlsxResponse(writer_klass=CalendarWriter)
        response.process(context)
        return response
```

issue_title: New feature: boolean operator (e.g. or, and) overloads for type bool
issue_body:
Hello István,

is it possible to add boolean operator overloads for py-flags?

I would like to use this syntax:

```Python
def _ExtractSimulationSteps(analyze, elaborate, optimize, recompile, simulate, resimulate, viewWaveform, guiMode):
  simulationSteps  =  SimulationSteps.No
  simulationSteps |=  SimulationSteps.Analyze &       analyze
  simulationSteps |=  SimulationSteps.Elaborate &     elaborate
  simulationSteps |=  SimulationSteps.Optimize &      optimize
  simulationSteps |=  SimulationSteps.Recompile &     recompile
  simulationSteps |=  SimulationSteps.Simulate &      simulate
  simulationSteps |=  SimulationSteps.Resimulate &    resimulate
  simulationSteps |=  SimulationSteps.ViewWaveform &  viewWaveform
  return simulationSteps
```
Note: Recompile is a combined flag of Analyze, Elaborate, Optimize.

Otherwise I would nee a lot of if statements:
```Python
def _ExtractSimulationSteps(analyze, elaborate, optimize, recompile, simulate, resimulate, viewWaveform, guiMode):
  simulationSteps = SimulationSteps.No
  if analyze:
    simulationSteps |= SimulationSteps.Analyze
  if elaborate:
    simulationSteps |= SimulationSteps.Elaborate
  if optimize:
    simulationSteps |= SimulationSteps.Optimize
  if recompile:
    simulationSteps |= SimulationSteps.Recompile
  if simulate:
    simulationSteps |= SimulationSteps.Simulate
  if resimulate:
    simulationSteps |= SimulationSteps.Resimulate
  if viewWaveform:
    simulationSteps |= SimulationSteps.ViewWaveform
  return simulationSteps
```

I'm thinking of this arithmetic rules:
* flag and true => flag
* flag and false => no_flags
* flag or bool => flag
* flag xor true => no_flags
* flag xor false => flag

Are other operations useful?

-------------------------
This is a local solution for my `SimulationSteps` class:

```Python
class SimulationSteps(Flags):
  """Simulation step enumeration."""
  CleanUp =        1 << 0
  Prepare =        1 << 1

  def __and__(self, other):
    if isinstance(other, bool):
      return self if other else self.__class__.no_flags
    else:
      return super().__and__(other)
```

issue_title: ThreadPoolExecutor resource cleanup?
issue_body:
When using `FuturesSession` for a long-running web scraper script, I've noticed a memory leak due to the fact that I wasn't cleaning up the `ThreadPoolExecutor`s that were created by the many `FuturesSession(max_workers=blah)` calls I was making.

I fixed the issue by writing a contextmanager that cleaned up my executor when exiting:
```python
@contextmanager
def clean_futures_session_when_done(session):
    try:
        yield
    finally:
        if session.executor:
            session.executor.shutdown()

with clean_futures_session_when_done(FuturesSession(max_workers=2)):
    do_stuff()
```
This feels a bit slimy since I'm using the internal(?) `self.executor` reference. I also realize that the `shutdown()` will block until all `Future`s are done, but I feel this is acceptable for many use cases.

An alternative I've considered is having `FuturesSession` implement the context manager protocol with `__enter__()` and `__exit__()` so we can directly use it in a with statement. This would be similar to how `open()` works:

```python
class FuturesSessionWithCleanup(FuturesSession):
    def __enter__(self):
        return self

    def __exit__(self, type, value, traceback):
        self.executor.shutdown()

with FuturesSessionWithCleanup(max_workers=2):
    do_stuff()
# block until all Futures are cleaned
```

Does this sound reasonable?

issue_title: Don't always call self.close() in __exit__()
issue_body:
`self.close()` should not be called when exiting with an exception.

Here's a specific real-world example using [Spotify's luigi framework](https://github.com/spotify/luigi).  Basically if an exception is raised then the output file is still partially written.

```python
import luigi.format
import newlinejson as nlj
import os


def cat(p):
    with open(p) as f:
        return f.read()


class NewlineJSONFormat(luigi.format.Format):

    input = 'dict'
    output = 'text'

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        self.args = args
        self.kwargs = kwargs
        if 'mode' in kwargs:
            raise ValueError("mode is auto-configured - can't supply as kwarg")

    def pipe_reader(self, input_pipe):
        return nlj.open(iter(input_pipe), mode='r', **self.kwargs.copy())

    def pipe_writer(self, output_pipe):
        return nlj.open(output_pipe, mode='w', **self.kwargs.copy())


path = 'TEST'

tgt = luigi.LocalTarget(path)

print("")
print("Write a file with a single line")
with tgt.open('w') as dst:
    dst.write('LINE')
print(cat(path))
os.remove(path)


print("")
print("Write a file with a single line but raise an exception, which causes the output file to not exist.")
try:
    with tgt.open('w') as dst:
        dst.write('LINE2')
        print("EXCEPTION")
        raise Exception
except Exception:
    pass
try:
    print(cat(path))
    os.remove(path)
except Exception:
    pass


print("")
print("Write a file with a single NLJ line")
nlj_tgt = luigi.LocalTarget(tgt.path, format=NewlineJSONFormat())
with nlj_tgt.open('w') as dst:
    dst.write({'key': 'value'})
print(cat(path))
os.remove(path)


print("")
print("Write a file with a single NLJ line but raise an exception like above")
try:
    with nlj_tgt.open('w') as dst:
        dst.write({'key': 'value'})
        print("EXCEPTION")
        raise Exception
except Exception:
    pass
try:
    print(cat(path))
    os.remove(path)
except Exception:
    pass
```

output:

```

Write a file with a single line
LINE

Write a file with a single line but raise an exception, which causes the output file to not exist.
EXCEPTION

Write a file with a single NLJ line
{"key": "value"}


Write a file with a single NLJ line but raise an exception like above
EXCEPTION
{"key": "value"}
```

issue_title: support to dump namedtuple objects
Submodule importing triggers recompile on each run
issue_body:
This occurs on python 2.7.12 and cppimport==17.7.24

I've created a little package to test submodule imports. The package `__init__.py` looks as follows:

```python
import cppimport

mod = cppimport.imp("testcppimport.qux.foobar")
plort = mod.qux.foobar.plort
```

Now if I invoke `plort` in multiple interpreter runs, `foobar.so` is recompiled, even though  it already exists.
```bash
python -c 'from testcppimport import plort; plort()'
cc1plus: warning: command line option ‘-Wstrict-prototypes’ is valid for C/ObjC but not for C++
Plort
python -c 'from testcppimport import plort; plort()'
cc1plus: warning: command line option ‘-Wstrict-prototypes’ is valid for C/ObjC but not for C++
Plort
```


Some debugging indicates that the call to [find_module](https://github.com/tbenthompson/cppimport/blob/98ab93726376830b61fc00aeef48b819b71a1852/cppimport/find.py#L66) fails with ImportError `No module named testcppimport.qux.foobar`. So the find fails, `foobar.so` is recompiled, loaded and `plort()` is invoked.

The test package is here [testcppimport.zip](https://github.com/tbenthompson/cppimport/files/1306397/testcppimport.zip)

issue_title: Don't call sys.exit from Args.__parse
issue_body:
By calling `sys.exit` directly from `Args.__parse`, which is called from the constructor you make it difficult for one to programmatically build args.

I would suggest instead having `__parse` raise an exception. This could be explicitly handled in any caller, the only one in your library itself being the CLI.

It would basically be changing 5 exit lines to raise Exceptions instead, and `ropgadget.main` to catch this exception. Instead of printing the error message directly you could of course also pass the message to the Exception's constructor and print it in `main`.

issue_title: PostgreSQL throws DataError when trying to retrieve objects with invalid UUID
issue_body:
The following code will trigger the problem with PostgreSQL backend:

````python
SomeModel.objects.get(uuid="invalid_uuid")
````

An example traceback:

````python
/.../lib/python2.7/site-packages/django/db/backends/utils.pyc in execute(self, sql, params)
     63                 return self.cursor.execute(sql)
     64             else:
---> 65                 return self.cursor.execute(sql, params)
     66 
     67     def executemany(self, sql, param_list):

DataError: invalid input syntax for uuid: "invalid_uuid"
LINE 1: ... "some_model" WHERE "some_model"."uuid" = 'invalid_u...
                                                     ^
````

PostgreSQL has a UUID type and throws an error with badly formatted UUID. However, it is not the expected behavior of an API. When user provides an invalid UUID, the API in most cases is expected to return 404, instead of 500 caused by this uncaught exception.

The problem lies in method [`get_db_prep_value`](https://github.com/dcramer/django-uuidfield/blob/57f6e43bf6f45a43cc15e4807f07c6bf92a3a69a/uuidfield/fields.py#L113-L122). If the provided UUID is not valid, the method should return an arbitrary valid UUID that matches nothing in the database.

issue_title: Implement automatic translation from pytz to Windows timezone names
issue_body:
The `PYTZ_TO_MS_MAP` hack in `exchangelib.ewsdatetime` is used to translate a Python (pytz) timezone name to a timezone name as expected by EWS, but it ishopelessly incomplete.

We should re-use the translations provided by CIDR, either by contributing an implementation to the `l18n` package (see issue https://bitbucket.org/tkhyn/l18n/issues/3/implement-pytz-to-windows-tz-translation) or by consuming the database directly: http://unicode.org/repos/cldr/trunk/common/supplemental/windowsZones.xml

`MS_TIMEZONE_DEFINITIONS` should either disappear or be replaced by a lazy `services.GetServerTimeZones` call.

issue_title: VersionAdapter should handle Version.revert()
issue_body:
It'd be useful if `VersionAdapter` handled `Version.revert()` to support more complex version handling.

If I want to adjust how version data is calculated and saved (in my case I want to calculate and store diffs) it's pretty easy to override methods in `VersionAdapter`, which is great. However adjusting the other half of the operation -- revert -- is difficult because modifying the behaviour or `Version` isn't supported and there's no extension point. If `Version` called back to the appropriate `VersionAdapter` we could place all the new logic, both version creation and revert code, in VersionAdapter.

Another way of looking at this is that the current implementation isn't symmetrical: VersionAdapter does all the hard work dealing with versions and Version is (almost always) treated as a naive data object. The `revert` call is the exception where suddenly `VersionAdapter` has no say and `Version` handles all the business logic.

My straw-man implementation looks something like this. It's a small change but would be a powerful extension point for more complex implementations of VersionAdapter handling.

```python
class Version(object):
    # ...
    def revert(self):
        default_revision_manager.get_adapter(self.get_model_type).revert(self)

class VersionAdapter(object):
    # ...
    def revert(self, version):
        version.object_version.save()
```

Or did I miss something and is this actually easy? :)

issue_title: Numpy arrays written without matlab metadata are transposed when read back when options not explicitly passed
issue_body:
If one takes a Numpy ndarray (or matrix) and writes it to an HDF5 file with python metadata but not Matlab metadata and reads the data back, the array is distorted unless options for ``hdf5storage.read`` are explicitly set to prevent it. If the array is square, it is transposed when it is read back. If the array has some other shape, the order of the elements gets jumbled up (transposed and then reshaped to the original shape).

As an example

```` python
>>> import numpy as np
>>> import hdf5storage
>>> a = np.array([[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6]])
>>> hdf5storage.write(a,path='/a',matlab_compatible=False,store_python_metadata=True)
>>> hdf5storage.read(path='/a')
array([[1, 4, 2],
       [5, 3, 6]])
````

Passing on ``matlab_compatible=False`` directly to ``hdf5storage.read`` fixes the problem.

```` python
>>> hdf5storage.read(path='/a',matlab_compatible=False)
array([[1, 2, 3],
       [4, 5, 6]])
````

Essentially, ``hdf5storage.read`` has ``matlab_compatible=True`` by default for its options (and thus ``reverse_dimension_order = True``). This causes a transpose if python metadata is present before being reshaped back to the right shape. If Matlab metadata was stored, it was transposed when writing meaning it gets transformed back properly. Only when python metadata but not matlab metadata is included does this happen.

This never came up in the test code because ``options`` is explicitly generated and passed explicitly to both ``hdf5storage.write`` and ``hdf5storage.read``.

I am going to fix this by making it so that ``matlab_compatible=False`` by default for reading data.

issue_title: Use __str__ for pretty-printing entries, and __repr__ for object representation
issue_body:
[Documentation for `__repr__`](https://docs.python.org/2/library/functions.html#repr):
> this function makes an attempt to return a string that would yield an object with the same value when passed to eval()

[Documentation for `__str__`](https://docs.python.org/2/library/functions.html#str):
> Return a string containing a nicely printable representation of an object.

Currently, calling `str()` or `print` on a `ChartData` invokes `__repr__` since `__str__` is not implemented.

Instead, `__repr__` could return a representation of the chart object (with corrected dates), and the current code can be used for `__str__`. Maybe the following the behavior:

    > chart = billboard.ChartData('hot-100', date='1996-07-30')
    > print repr(chart)
    billboard.ChartData('hot-100', date='1996-08-03')
    > print chart
    hot-100 chart from 1996-08-03
    -----------------------------
    1. ‘Macarena (Bayside Boys Mix)’ by Los Del Rio
    ...

Should be an easy fix. Let me know if this is a good idea, and I can go ahead and implement it.

issue_title: msgpack not unpack unicode string correclty - should be unicode but is str
issue_body:
It leads to problems since after such conversion - us.encode() is valid but decoded.encode() is invalid ('utf-8' - python 2.7.x). Type should be not changed during serialization.

```
import msgpack

us = u'Hello World!'
s = us.encode()

encoded = msgpack.packb(us)
decoded = msgpack.unpackb(encoded)

print type(us), type(decoded)

encoded = msgpack.packb(s)
decoded = msgpack.unpackb(encoded)

print type(s), type(decoded)
```

issue_title: Specify requires_python in setup.py

issue_title: api.inherit() not working as intended Swagger allOf Feature
issue_body:
It seems like the api.inherit() / model.inherit() function does not seem perform the same affect on the Rest API as the Swagger allOf feature, which I believe is the intended functionality of the inherit function. 
Specifically, I don't think the discriminator value works as Swagger docs intend it to.

Looking at swagger's documentation (https://swagger.io/docs/specification/data-models/oneof-anyof-allof-not/#allof), we see that when you specify allOf in a certain model that references a parent model, then the name of the child model is used as a reference for the discriminator value. In my flask-restplus implementation, the value of the discriminator field doesn't have any affect on the allowed fields. For example, if I had:
```
pet = api.model('Pet', {
  'type': field.String(required=True, discriminator=True)
})

dog = api.inherit('Dog', pet, {
  'dogType': field.String(required=True)
})
```
then the API would accept a request body like so: `{'type': 'Dog'}` even though it should respond that the `dogType` field is missing.

Also, it seems that in the example in the swagger dos, the discriminator value can only be either Cat or Dog and nothing else. However, in flask-restplus, I can set it equal to anything with no effect. For example, using the models above, the API would also accept something like `{'type': 'Toaster'}`. Is this supposed to be the intended functionality?

Flask-Restplus is really helpful for designing Swagger API's dynamically for Python, but the allOf description and the discriminator field are both really important features that I would like to make sure is working correct. If you can, could you please describe what you think about this? Thanks!

        """

        c_data, address = sock.recvfrom(8129)
        
        # creates a myThread object that is a derived class from threading.Thread

        # Runs the thread

```
`newthread.start()` runs a function that processes `c_data`  and emits events to connected clients.

issue_title: Highlight extension should apply to indented code blocks
issue_body:
I don't like how CodeHilite limits what can be on the first line.  So I don't think Highlight will ever adopt the mock shebang idea for code hilghighting.  In InlineHilite, you can escape with a space as leading and trailing spaces are stripped out anyways, but with indented code blocks, there isn't really any way to do so.  I feel like fences are just a better suited way to do these kind of things.

With all that said, I think highlight should at least be feeding indented code blocks through highlight for consistency.  If `guess_lang` is False, the blocks will just be styled as plain text, but if `guess_lang` is `True`, then the blocks will actually get highlighted.  Same goes for global line number options.  But if a user wishes to fine tune things, or manually apply settings in the header of a code block, they should be using SuperFences or Fences etc.

issue_title: Using of default instead of initial to pre-populate html fields
issue_body:
Hi, 

It seems that swagger is using the "default" attribute instead of the "initial" value to prepopulate the html fields.

If I have a field like this `quantity = IntegerField(default=1)`, it gets rendered as:
```<input class="parameter" minlength="0" name="quantity" placeholder="" value="1" type="text">```. the field becomes "non requrired".

And if my field is `quantity = IntegerField(initial=1)`, it gets rendered as:
```<input class="parameter required" minlength="1" name="quantite" placeholder="(required)" value="" type="text">```. The initial value is not taken into account.

According to [django rest docs](http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/fields/#core-arguments), "initial" attribute is:
> A value that should be used for pre-populating the value of HTML form fields.

while "default" is:
> If set, this gives the default value that will be used for the field if no input value is supplied. [...] 
Note that setting a default value implies that the field is not required. Including both the default and required keyword arguments is invalid and will raise an error.

I would like my field to be required and have and "html" default value of 1.

Is this a bug of expected behavior for swagger? If it's a bug, please just point me in the right direction and I'll correct it and make a pull request.

Thanks

issue_title: ^ support isn't npm-compatible
issue_body:
npm treats ^ differently for 0.x versions, see the node-semver docs: https://github.com/npm/node-semver#caret-ranges-123-025-004

(and fwiw yotta also treats it [differently](http://yottadocs.mbed.com/reference/module.html#dependencies), treating ^ for 0.x versions as meaning exact version, as semver technically has no compatibility guarantees for 0.x versions)

(While I was checking the code i also noticed a double-return here: https://github.com/rbarrois/python-semanticversion/blob/master/semantic_version/base.py#L462-L463)

issue_title: Python 2.6 mode
issue_body:
It would be nice if a project can specify that they need '2.6' support, and only rules that dont break 2.6 support would be active.

For example, the set rules in #2 rely on Python 2.7 syntax.
As a result, we need to turn off those codes, despite those codes having many good rules that are applicable and valid on Python 2.6, because a few rules are not.

https://gerrit.wikimedia.org/r/#/c/302103/

It would be especially lovely if you supported `min-version = 2.6` that [flake8-future-import](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/flake8-future-import) uses, which also allows for other versions to have specific syntax that affects these rules.

Django system check warning on URL route entry
issue_body:
The current README suggests the following urls.py entry:
```
url(r'^ht/$', include('health_check.urls')),
```
However, running `django-admin check` then yields:
```
System check identified some issues:

WARNINGS:
?: (urls.W001) Your URL pattern '^ht/$' uses include with a regex ending with a '$'. Remove the dollar from the regex to avoid problems including URLs.
```
Removing the dollar sign caused the warning to go away, with no apparent ill effects regarding django-health-check app functionality.

issue_title: UX improvements to 'unsaved changes'
issue_body:
Currently a system modal dialog is used to tell users to save changes before saving.

In my previous home-brewed solution to this, I instead updated a hidden order field so that the new ordering was only saved when the modal itself was saved. In some ways I prefer your approach but I does create introduce a new problem that you've used this modal to solve.

Modals are a bit unpleasant in terms of UX - both visually and the fact you have to click to dismiss them. I've got a few thoughts:

1. Something less obstructive - a brief position:fixed flash message that fades out?
2. Is there a way to avoid this entirely? Could the new ordering be written to the db in such a way that it doesn't matter that there are unsaved changes? I'm guessing behind the scenes you're not doing queryset.update probably to avoid bypassing instance.save() However - would it be safe to update a single field and thus relax the restriction on unsaved changes?

issue_title: Add an infinite feed implementation
issue_body:
The original feed implementation's intent seemed to be that of an infinite feed where if a `last_seq` was encountered as part of a continuous feed then another http request would be initiaited to the feed endpoint using the last_seq value as the "since" value thus creating an infinite feed iterator.

Since the feed was rewritten (as part of #45) to be more in line with how the `_changes` and `_db_updates` feed endpoints and their options function, this infinite feed functionality has been somewhat lost.

This issue tracks the re-addition of the infinite feed functionality back into this library.

Consider an InfiniteFeed class??

## Error Message:

MXNetError                                Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-23-133db28d92b2> in <module>()
----> 1 mx.nd.save("s3://imagetraining.xxxx.com/testing/a",[a,b])

/home/ubuntu/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/mxnet-0.7.0-py2.7.egg/mxnet/ndarray.pyc in save(fname, data)
   1082                                   mx_uint(len(handles)),
   1083                                   c_array(NDArrayHandle, handles),
-> 1084                                   keys))
   1085 
   1086 def imdecode(str_img, clip_rect=(0, 0, 0, 0), out=None, index=0, channels=3, mean=None):

/home/ubuntu/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/mxnet-0.7.0-py2.7.egg/mxnet/base.pyc in check_call(ret)
     75     """
     76     if ret != 0:
---> 77         raise MXNetError(py_str(_LIB.MXGetLastError()))
     78 
     79 if sys.version_info[0] < 3:

MXNetError: [03:32:57] src/io/s3_filesys.cc:682: Check failed: num_retry < max_error_retry_  maximum retry time reached

## Minimum reproducible example
if you are using your own code, please provide a short script that reproduces the error.

Create a new Jyputer notebook, run the following Python , make sure that the bucket name of S3 has period in it: 

a = mx.nd.zeros((100, 200))
b = a+1
mx.nd.save("s3://imagetraining.xxx.com/testing/a",[a,b])




## Steps to reproduce
or if you are running standard examples, please provide the commands you have run that lead to the error.

1. Start Jupyter.
2. Create a notebook with the sample code above 
3. Run it and then will fail

## What have you tried to solve it?

1. Tried another S3 bucket without period in the bucket name. It worked.
2. In the document of S3: http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/dev/VirtualHosting.html, found the following description:

When using virtual hosted–style buckets with SSL, the SSL wild card certificate only matches buckets that do not contain periods. To work around this, use HTTP or write your own certificate verification logic.



3. Looked into the source code dmlc-core/src/io/s3_filesys.cc:682, found that the source code is using virtual host style url to access S3, as the following code shows:

surl << "https://" << path_.host << ".s3.amazonaws.com" << '/'

This virtual host style method will fail because of SSL issue when the target bucket has period in the bucket name.

issue_title: Call symbol/nd.smooth_l1 without scalar causing uncaught exception
issue_body:
Call mx.nd.smooth_l1 without scalar parameter will cause mxnet to crash
```
libc++abi.dylib: terminating with uncaught exception of type std::invalid_argument: stod: no conversion
```

## Steps to reproduce
or if you are running standard examples, please provide the commands you have run that lead to the error.

```
import mxnet.ndarray as nd
nd.smooth_l1(nd.array([1, 2]))
```

## What have you tried to solve it?

Specify scalar will solve it
```
nd.smooth_l1(nd.array([1, 2]), scalar=1.0)
```
However, in the example, the argument is specified as `sigma`, which is very confusing.
http://mxnet.io/api/python/symbol.html#mxnet.symbol.smooth_l1

We should add a default value for scalar (1.0), fix the document, and fix the uncaught exception anyway

issue_title: Retry with back off for 500 errors
issue_body:
Right now the script will exit if it receives a sever-busy response: `(500): {"error_message": "Sorry, this request could not be processed. Please try again later."}`

We should add automatic retries for an HTTP 500 response, with some sort of reasonable back off so we don't continually hammer the server when it is busy. As it is now we have to manually relaunch the script every time.

issue_title: Running zmq.SUB in multiprocessing.Process
issue_body:
I have come across a strange behaviour when I create a zmq.SUB listener socket (i.e., `socket.bind`) from multiprocessing.Process. The subprocess receives messages fine, but when it ends and closes the socket, and I then start another Process from the same Python session, the second instance doesn’t receive any messages from clients. It binds to the socket,  starts listening and is even shown by `netstat` as owning the socket, but it never returns from `socket.recv`. 

This happens on both Linux and OSX, Python 2.7 and 3.6, and with both ipc and TCP sockets.

Pyzmq 16.0.2 and zmq 4.1.6.

Code to reproduce:

```python
import os
import zmq
import time
from multiprocessing import Process as Process

addr = 'tcp://127.0.0.1:2344'
# addr = 'ipc://foo'
# addr = 'ipc://@foo'
def server(addr):
    print("Start server")
    print('parent process: %d' % os.getppid())
    print('process id: %d' % os.getpid())
    context = zmq.Context.instance()
    sub = context.socket(zmq.SUB)
    sub.linger = 0
    sub.subscribe('')
    sub.bind(addr)
    time.sleep(.1)
    print(sub.recv())
    sub.close()
    print("Server done")

def client(addr, msg):
    print("Start client")
    context = zmq.Context.instance()
    sub = context.socket(zmq.PUB)
    sub.linger = 0
    sub.connect(addr)
    print("Connected...")
    time.sleep(1)
    sub.send(msg)
    print("Closing client...")
    sub.close()

listener = Process(target = server, args = (addr, ))
listener.start()
time.sleep(.2)

client(addr, b"hello there!")
listener.join()
time.sleep(3)

print("Start again")
listener = Process(target = server, args = (addr, ))
listener.start()
time.sleep(.2)

client(addr, b"hello again!")

listener.join()
print("Done")
```

issue_title: socket not lingering even with explicit call to term
issue_body:
I read #149, but it's overly specific in this case. I'm not using a ProcessDevice. I'm sending a multipart message via a PUB socket, just before exiting a process. However, the message is never received by the SUB socket, unless I use a sleep statement after calling send. 

#149 seems to explains why this happens. However, calling term() explicitly should block until all messages have been sent, per the ZMQ documentation. But this doesn't happen. This is a small message, so I'm surprised that ZMQ fails to send it fast enough.

Is there a way to ensure that everything has been sent before exiting? I really dislike using sleep statements, as it just feels like a dirty hack.

issue_title: We should explain how ID token refreshment and JWT Expiration works
issue_body: I can imagine that it's really confusing to people how the refresh ID token works and how it coincides with JWT Expiration. By default, in Auth0, the JWT expiration is 3600 seconds. That means that you have to do an ID token refresher at least every 3600 or it ceases to be valid and the user gets kicked out. (Thanks to our middleware). I don't know why Auth0 uses such a low default. Perhaps it's because the concept of constantly refreshing the ID token is optional. And it's set to 3600 just for convenience on the first use. Only with enterprise emails can you "abruptly" lose the right to your email. (Unlike a '@gmail.com` which you won't lose). Generally, I think the trick is to make sure this number is quite high. In Air Mozilla I think we set it to a week (604800). Meaning, they have to visit the site at least once a week or else they have to sign in again. I for one took a long time to understand how this works. Our documentation is a chance to help others. Especially people who want to use the RefreshIDToken middleware.

issue_title: Issues with our Elastic Load Balancer
issue_body:
Hello,

we're using Riak over protocol buffers, and with Amazon's Elastic Load Balancer in the middle. We're seeing these exceptions pop out:

```
Error while storing avatar to Riak: 'Socket returned short packet length 0 - expected 4'
```

(Here's another person with a similar issue: http://codersdiscuss.com/4870/timeout-issues-with-the-riak-python-customer-with-process-buffers/.)

As far as we can see, it's the idle timeout setting of the ELB that's the problem - a worker can be idle for more than the idle timeout, and ELB will close some (or both - to the worker and to Riak) of the connections. Then, the next Riak action will result in the above exception.

We will probably deal with this on the application layer. However, it'd be very convenient if this could be dealt with on the pool layer transparently.

According to Python docs (https://docs.python.org/2/howto/sockets.html):
```
When a recv returns 0 bytes, it means the other side has closed (or is in the process of closing) the connection. You will not receive any more data on this connection. Ever.
```

The RiakPbcPool class already has a set of exceptions on which to retry calls. How about adding this particular case to the set of auto-retry scenarios?

issue_title: Local scheduler does not prune automatically
issue_body:
Take these script: a main task that depends on a task that only completes successfully after some time has passed since launch
```python
import time
import luigi

class DepThatNeedsSomeTime(luigi.Task):
    ready_time = luigi.FloatParameter()

    def run(self):
        assert self.complete()

    def complete(self):
        return  self.ready_time <= time.time()

class MainTask(luigi.WrapperTask):
    launch_time = luigi.FloatParameter(default=time.time())

    def requires(self):
        return [DepThatNeedsSomeTime(self.launch_time + 10)]

if __name__ == '__main__':
    luigi.run()
```

run it with these configs to simplify testing/debugging:
```
python script.py MainTask --scheduler-retry-delay 1 --worker-keep-alive
```

If you run this with central scheduler, the dependency will fail initially, but after some it will be retried because the server calls `scheduler.prune` every minute:
```python
    # prune work DAG every 60 seconds
    pruner = tornado.ioloop.PeriodicCallback(scheduler.prune, 60000)
```
and `scheduler.prune` causes rescheduling/retrying of the failed task (through triggering `SimpleTaskState.prune`)
 
So eventually all work will be done successfully.


However if you run this with the local scheduler (additional `--local-scheduler`), you end up in an endless loop because `scheduler.prune` is never called and the failed task never gets a chance on a retry.

(FYI: I lost quite some time figuring all this out when I was examining the retry behaviour of Luigi with the local scheduler, assuming it would work the same as the central scheduler)

issue_title: Unexpected behaviour when using luigi.build()
issue_body:
Hi,

We've been trying to use the `build` functionality in order to execute a set of tasks within a task itself. Our use-case is that we want to have some "monitoring" tasks that run after a task has run without adding it has a dependency. 

Given the following example:

```
Class MyS3CopyToTable(redshift.S3CopyToTable, ...):
    def run(self):
        """
        If the target table doesn't exist, self.create_table
        will be called to attempt to create the table.
        """
        if not (self.table):
            raise Exception("table need to be specified")

        path = self.s3_load_path()
        output = self.output()
        connection = output.connect()
        cursor = connection.cursor()

        self.init_copy(connection)
        self.copy(cursor, path)
        self.post_copy(cursor)

        # update marker table
        output.touch(connection)
        connection.commit()

        # commit and clean up
        connection.close()

        # This is what we've added
        luigi.build([myMonitoringTask(params)])

class MyTask(MyS3CopyToTable)
  ...
```

If we were to run `MyTask` with one worker (`--workers=1`), using the central scheduler, then everything executes fine, and my built task (`myMonitoringTask`) gets executed successfully.

On the other hand, if I were to run it with multiple workers (`--workers=2`), I see that it spawns my `myMonitoringTask` correctly but it just hangs.

issue_title: FBX import scene hierarchy is broken by unnamed nodes
issue_body:
I briefly tested the commit 6a0dc071a967be9d69ed86228f21be04dfec23d6 cloned on April 9th 2018, and had many problems with FBX import and hierarchies. These problems are not present in the latest release 4.1.0.

I was importing mostly FBX files, and it seems that with deeper hierarchies Assimp would insert unnamed parent nodes containing the transform of their child, and then their child node would have the node name (as written in 3DS Max or Maya).

This was especially visible with animations where each bone would have an unnamed parent (containing the transform). But it also happens on files which don't have animations.

So for most deep nodes we get this:
> The usual $AssimpFbx$_PreRotation etc. nodes
> Some parent which has a proper name and transform
>----unnamed parent node with transform
>-------- its child node, which has the node name, but identity transform

I'm not sure if this is some new feature, or just broken stuff in master, but it would be nice to know if this can be fixed, or if not, the reasons for the change.

If this is a bug, I would expect the hierarchy to roughly match the hierarchy that was set in 3DSMax or Maya, and not contain those unnamed parent nodes, like it does in Assimp 4.1.0.

issue_title: OpenGEXImporter: Segfault when LightObject is not referenced from LightNode
issue_body:
Hello everybody,

I am integrating assimp for https://github.com/mosra/magnum-plugins/ and found the OpenGEXImporter to crash on our [test file for lights](https://github.com/mosra/magnum-plugins/blob/master/src/MagnumPlugins/OpenGexImporter/Test/light.ogex).

The issue here is that the LightObject structures are not referenced by any LightNode, but the `m_currentLight` member is initialized only on encounter of a LightNode [here](https://github.com/assimp/assimp/blob/master/code/OpenGEXImporter.cpp#L576), whereas handling the LightObject will try to set `m_currentLight->mType` [here](https://github.com/assimp/assimp/blob/master/code/OpenGEXImporter.cpp#L610-L616), even if unreferenced.

I checked the OpenGEX documentation to make sure that our file is valid, and while a LightNode is required to contain a reference to a LightObject structure, a LightObject is not required to be referenced by anything.

Cheers, Jonathan.

issue_title: Raising exception in a pre-commit hook doesn't abort the transaction
issue_body:
Since pyramid_tm 2.0, raising an exception in a pre-commit hook no longer marks the transaction as aborted. I've been trying to track down exactly what's going on, and I think there are two different things in 2.0 and 2.1. In 2.0, it seems like my transaction is getting retried somehow, and I'm not sure why. However, I'm more interested in porting to 2.1, so let's focus on that. It seems that as a result of https://github.com/Pylons/pyramid_tm/pull/61, the exception is caught and rendered. However, this does not cause the transaction to be aborted.

We were relying on the old behavior, where raising during pre-commit causes the transaction to abort, in Kinto. Our workaround (in https://github.com/Kinto/kinto/pull/1263) is to manually call `abort()` before we raise, but this seems a little strange. I'm hoping for advice on how to best accomplish our goals :)

Is it bad for us to even use a pre-commit hook in this way? Should we maybe be using a pre-commit veto or something instead? Personally, I feel that raising in a pre-commit should still abort the transaction. Another approach might be to abort the transaction in the `invoke_exception_view`, but we don't have any customizations of that so far and I don't know if now is the right time to add any.

Thanks for your time!

issue_title: After vMotion VmMigrated event the VM's VNIC has stale portgroup data for a period of time.
issue_body:
Using pyvmomi version 6.5 and vSphere Version 6.5.0 Build 4944578

I have a listener that is responding to a `vim.event.VmMigratedEvent` to determine that a vMotion has completed.  Using the event data, I need to determine the portgroup for each VNIC on the VM that moved to the new host.

I'm currently interrogating the event as follows:

```python
for device in vm_event.vm.vm.config.hardware.device:
    portgroup = device.backing.deviceName
```
This works fine to extract the portgroup, except that If I vMotion a VM back and forth, then after the second migration this data is always stale (it always refers to the source host).  After a period of time, the portgroup data is then refreshed, but not in time for event processing.

I tried fetching the data directly for the VM (not using the event view) with the following method, but still the data is stale.

```python
            vm = vsphere_connection.vsphere_connection.get_object_by_property(
                property_name='name',
                property_value=virtual_machine.name,
                obj_type=vim.VirtualMachine
            )
            self._logger.debug('VM = %s', vm)
            for vnic in vm.config.hardware.device:
                self._logger.debug('VM vnic %s', vnic)
                if vnic:
                    if vnic.backing:
                        try:
                            self._logger.debug('vnic backing = %s', vnic.backing)
                            if vnic.backing.deviceName:
                                self._logger.debug('vnic backing device = %s', vnic.backing.deviceName)
                        except AttributeError:
                            pass
```

I found that by adding a sleep for 10 seconds after the `vim.event.VmMigratedEvent` is received, but before processing the event at all, that the data was then populated correctly.  I'd prefer not to add an arbitrary delay, but I don't see a programatic way that I can detect when the data is refreshed.

Any suggestions?  Is there a backend bug here that is allowing the event to be sent before all the processing is complete?

The reason I expect this is because that's the way `source .env` works in Bash, and that's also the way [`python-dotenv`](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/python-dotenv) works.


##### Actual result

```
$ pipenv run env | grep -i foo
Loading .env environment variables…
FOO_ONE=FOO_TWO=
```

The actual result was that the environment variable `FOO_ONE` was set to `FOO_TWO=`, and the environment variable `FOO_TWO` was not created.

issue_title: package_dir and setup.cfg version attr: <foo> mutually exclusive?
issue_body:
I was messing around with the new ``setup.cfg`` support for metadata, and it looks like there is a poor interaction with ``package_dir`` and the version field support, specifically if you have a ``setup.cfg`` like:

```
[metadata]
version = attr: foobar.__version__

[options]
package_dir =
    = src
```

You'll get an error trying to import foobar. This is because it simply appends the current directory to ``sys.path`` and then attempts to import the above.

issue_body:

issue_title: Reject invalid distribution package names
issue_body: As revealed in pypa/pip#3681, Setuptools allows package names that are invalid according to [PEP 426](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0426/). Setuptools should issue a warning at build time about invalid package names and eventually reject invalid package names.

issue_title: Remove non-python dependencies
issue_body:
- [ ] wget: use [wget from pypi](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/wget) instead of /usr/{,local/}bin/wget

- [ ] bgzip (for `augmented_pheno_gz`):

issue_title: Support passing additional options to getLastError
issue_body:
getLastError supports multiple options (see http://www.mongodb.org/display/DOCS/getLastError+Command#getLastErrorCommand-Options), but providing these options are currently not possible in txmongo.

This is supported by pymongo by passing extra keyword arguments to the insert/update/remove commands. Default options are possible to set on both the database and collection instances. It would be great having this feature here.

issue_title: API resize/rescale redundant?
issue_body:
## Description
Any difference between the two API, [`skimage.transform.rescale`](http://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/api/skimage.transform.html#rescale) vs [`skimage.transform.resize`](http://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/api/skimage.transform.html#resize)?  It seems to me having both is redundant and confusing.  And as for code, `rescale` is just a thin wrapper around `resize`.

Concretely the only difference between `rescale` and `resize` is the parameter type, i.e., `float` vs `int`.

One possible way to combine the `rescale` and `resize` is allowing both type of inputs.  For example, [`pandas.DataFrame.sample(n=None, frac=None, ...)`](http://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/generated/pandas.DataFrame.sample.html) takes two mutually exclusive parameters `n` and `frac`, which is similar in the case of `rescale` and `resize`.

issue_body:
Hi,

yesterday I've noticed that there is a bug in the Document File Manager that allows user to upload a file with /,(,),. characters in the file name encoded using URL-encoding, for example "art%3A10.1007%2FJHEP01%282013%29057.pdf".
Aside from the fact that if those characters weren't encoded, Document File Manager wouldn't allow to upload files like that (at least not with front-slash in the file name), after a user uploads a file like that then we can have multiple problems:
- If a user deletes the file then the bibupload:FFT task with FIX-MARC is failing with error: `Stage 2B failed: ERROR: while synchronizing 8564 tags: invalid literal for int() with base 10: '12345678/art:10.1007`. It's caused by this function `decompose_file(urllib.unquote(recid_file))`
- Probably the same error might occur when a user is uploading more files to that record.
- Some format elements are not creating a correct URL to the file, for example Default_HTML_files.bft is creating link with decoded URL characters (so something like that: `/record/12345678/files/art:10.1007/JHEP01(2013)057.pdf` and that returns 404 instead of a file).

Tibor mentioned that someone was already working on patching that ?

Originally by *[adeiana (@Osso)](https://github.com/Osso)* on 2014-02-24

Currently situation:

I propose this solution:

2) We should modify the print_record() to return a filtered recstruct. That is needed because some fields are protected and should not exposed to the user.
