issue_title: Change method signature for turicreate.text_analytics.trim_rare_words(....)
issue_body:
`turicreate.text_analytics.trim_rare_words` has a `stopwords` parameter. These are words that will always be dropped or "trimmed". Stop list words are not rare words, in fact they are typically the most common words. So the function name should probably not have the word "rare" in it.

I also think "drop" is a better description than "trim". 

The `stopwords` parameter should be `stop_words`.

Default values for this method should also be reexamined:
- I'm not sure it makes sense for `thereshold` to have a hard coded default value. It just seems too dependent on your data size and word distribution. We could either make the user specify the threshold or have `"auto"` as a threshold and do something smart based on the data.
- Rather than having the default value for `stopwords` be `None`, I think it should be set to our set of stop words (i.e. `tc.text_analytics.stopwords()`).

If anyone has thoughts here, please share.

issue_title: Add setup.py and release on Pypi
myth -x returns "input files do not contain any valid contracts"
Related resources don't obtain object via the resource_uri's resource
issue_body:
tl;dr: The type of object that a resource_uri points to is not asserted to be the same as the type of the foreign key.

```python
class FooResource(ModelResource):
  class Meta:
    resource_name = 'foo'
    queryset = Foo.objects.all()

class BarResource(ModelResource):
  class Meta:
    resource_name = 'bar'
    queryset = Bar.objects.all()

class BazResource(ModelResource):
  foo = ForeignKey(FooResource, 'foo')

  class Meta:
    always_return_data = True
    authentication = Authentication()
    authorization = Authorization()
    list_allowed_methods = ('post',)
    resource_name = 'baz'
    queryset = Baz.objects.all()
  ...
```

When assigning to the `foo` attribute of `BazResource` via a resource_uri, you can pass any resource_uri as the value (not necessarily a resource_uri to the `FooResource`), as long as it has the same non-resource-name kwargs as `FooResource`. This comes down to the implementation of `get_via_uri`:

```python
      def get_via_uri(self, uri, request=None):
        ...
        try:
            view, args, kwargs = resolve(chomped_uri)
        except Resolver404:
            raise NotFound(u"The URL provided '%s' was not a link to a valid resource." % uri)

        bundle = self.build_bundle(request=request)
        return self.obj_get(bundle=bundle, **self.remove_api_resource_names(kwargs))
```

If I supply `/api/bar/1/` as the value of the `foo` attribute to `BazResource`, the kwargs are extracted as `{'pk': '1', 'api_name': 'api', 'resource_name': 'bar'}`. However, `obj_get` is then called on `self`, which is a `FooResource` instance. At no point is the resource name verified to be correct.

```bash
$ curl -v -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' --data '{"foo": "/api/bar/1/"}' http://localhost:8000/api/baz/
* About to connect() to localhost port 8000 (#0)
*   Trying 127.0.0.1...
* connected
* Connected to localhost (127.0.0.1) port 8000 (#0)
> POST /api/baz/ HTTP/1.1
> User-Agent: curl/7.24.0 (x86_64-apple-darwin12.0) libcurl/7.24.0 OpenSSL/0.9.8r zlib/1.2.5
> Host: localhost:8000
> Accept: */*
> Content-Type: application/json
> Content-Length: 22
>
* upload completely sent off: 22 out of 22 bytes
* HTTP 1.0, assume close after body
< HTTP/1.0 201 CREATED
< Date: Fri, 31 May 2013 05:28:07 GMT
< Server: WSGIServer/0.1 Python/2.7.2
< Vary: Accept, Accept-Language, Cookie
< Content-Type: application/json
< Location: http://localhost:8000/api/baz/25/
< Content-Language: en
<
* Closing connection #0
{"foo": "/api/foo/1/", "resource_uri": "/api/baz/25/"}
```

Is this by design or is it a bug? I can't see anything anywhere obvious that's indicating that this is by design.

issue_title: add forecasting tools to pvlib
issue_body:
Hello fellow pvlib-ers,

I've found some funding to develop open source solar power forecasting tools based on pvlib python and US weather models. EPRI and Southern Company will fund a U. Arizona grad student, Derek Groenendyk, (@MoonRaker) to do the majority of the work this fall semester, though I will work closely with Derek on the project and I will maintain it in the future. The hope is that the pvlib-based forecasts can be reproducible and documentable benchmarks for the solar power forecasting community.

My plan is to add a new ``forecasting.py`` module to pvlib python, though I think we should have a conversation about if it's better to add it as a separate project under the pvlib organization. If the community is opposed to this too, then we will host it on our UA-REN organization account.

Unidata is putting together a nice project called [Siphon](https://github.com/Unidata/siphon) that makes it relatively easy to access data from a number of US weather models (though some cover more than just the US). I experimented with Siphon in [this notebook](http://nbviewer.ipython.org/github/wholmgren/siphon/blob/solar_testing/examples/notebooks/solar_testing.ipynb) and concluded that this project was very feasible.

One thing that we will need to do is come to some sort of agreement on how to spec out PV systems in pvlib. This has been discussed in a number of other issues in pvlib including #84 #17. So, I think this project should benefit pvlib users even if they're not interested in solar power forecasts.

Thanks in advance for your input on this. 

I'll ping a few people just to make sure that they see this... @bmu @Calama-Consulting @jforbess

In [3]: times.dayofyear
Out[3]: Int64Index([175, 175, 175, 175], dtype='int64')

In [5]: pvlib.irradiance.extraradiation(times.dayofyear)
Out[5]: Float64Index([1321.16558348, 1321.16558348, 1321.16558348, 1321.16558348], dtype='float64')
```

Simply passing ``times`` (no ``.dayofyear``) will return a ``Series``, and everything works again. I don't know why I chose to use ``times.dayofyear`` instead of just ``times`` when building this method. Maybe ``irradiance.extraradiation`` didn't accept Series back then?

Next, the ``solarposition.ephemeris`` function contains

```python
    DayOfYear = time_utc.dayofyear
    DecHours = (time_utc.hour + time_utc.minute/60. + time_utc.second/3600. +
                time_utc.microsecond/3600.e6)
```

Wrapping these variables with calls to ``np.array`` should ensure they're the right type.

I'll make a PR soon.

issue_title: WagtailTranslationOptions doesn't find all Page models
issue_body:
In 0.6rc1, the new `WagtailTranslationOptions` applies the standard base fields to any model that derives from `Page`. However, I noticed a model that had multiple inheritances (specifically a form based on `WagtailCaptchaEmailForm` which is based on `AbstractEmailForm` which is based on `AbstractForm` which is based on `Page`), wasn't picked up by if options.

On Python 2.7, Django 1.8.13, `model.__bases__` only returns the direct parent of the Page model. I added `or Page in model._meta.get_parent_list()` which solved my problem.

So the entire if statement in WagtailTranslationOptions could read:
` if Page in model.__bases__ or Page in model._meta.get_parent_list():`

Or, perhaps there's a better way to get the base class of a model?

issue_title: BlockNode can be missing in Function block list
issue_body:
Hello, Apparently, in some code (code that has exceptions, or code that initializes hardware for example), we can encounter this kind of function call: ``` 00008008 : 8008: 4b08 ldr r3, [pc, #32] ; (802c ) 800a: 4809 ldr r0, [pc, #36] ; (8030 ) 800c: 4283 cmp r3, r0 800e: d20a bcs.n 8026 8010: 43da mvns r2, r3 8012: 2100 movs r1, #0 8014: 4402 add r2, r0 8016: f022 0203 bic.w r2, r2, #3 801a: 3204 adds r2, #4 801c: 441a add r2, r3 801e: f843 1b04 str.w r1, [r3], #4 8022: 4293 cmp r3, r2 8024: d1fb bne.n 801e 8026: f000 b83b b.w 80a0
``` Function `Main` is called in `ResetISR` using a `b` instruction instead of `bl`. Of course, VEX does not detect the call and `CFGAccurate` does not either. The problem might be just that there will not be any `Function` object for `Main`, and all basic blocks of `Main` will be in `Function` of `ResetISR`. But it can also be that a `Function` object does not contain any blocks. Here's an example: ``` 0000a220 : a220: 6041 str r1, [r0, #4] a222: 4770 bx lr ``` This function only has one single block. It is called from different places: - Here with a `b`, the function call is not detected ``` 0000969c : 969c: b538 push {r3, r4, r5, lr} 969e: 4605 mov r5, r0 96a0: 4c07 ldr r4, [pc, #28] ; (96c0 ) 96a2: 2200 movs r2, #0 96a4: 4620 mov r0, r4 96a6: 213d movs r1, #61 ; 0x3d 96a8: f000 fdae bl a208 96ac: 4629 mov r1, r5 96ae: 4620 mov r0, r4 96b0: f000 fdc4 bl a23c 96b4: 4620 mov r0, r4 96b6: e8bd 4038 ldmia.w sp!, {r3, r4, r5, lr} 96ba: 2103 movs r1, #3 96bc: f000 bdb0 b.w a220 ``` - Or here with a `bl`, but since the `BlockNode` is already in the `OSRAMWriteFirst` `Function` object, the `Function` `I2CMasterControl` is created but has no block. ``` 96ec: 2101 movs r1, #1 96ee: 4620 mov r0, r4 96f0: f000 fd96 bl a220 ``` So, I don't know what would be the best way to fix this. Adding more logic to reason about jumpkind? Or we can just add more test in `Function` class, like here. I guess this is a @ltfish issue. Please let me know what you think

issue_title: Incorrect handling of rep stosd
issue_body:
In some cases, when trying to lift a basic block which contains a *rep stosd* instruction, we get a wrong translation.
For example, when trying to lift "\xb8\xcc\xcc\xcc\xcc\xf3\xab":
```
mov eax, 0xcccccccc
rep stosd
```
we get:
```
00 | ------ IMark(0x0, 5, 0) ------
   01 | PUT(eax) = 0xcccccccc
   02 | PUT(eip) = 0x00000005
   03 | ------ IMark(0x5, 2, 0) ------
   04 | t1 = GET:I32(ecx)
   05 | t4 = CmpEQ32(t1,0x00000000)
   06 | if (t4) { PUT(eip) = 0x7; Ijk_Boring }
   07 | t5 = Sub32(t1,0x00000001)
   08 | PUT(ecx) = t5
   09 | t7 = GET:I32(dflag)
   10 | t6 = Shl32(t7,0x02)
   11 | t3 = GET:I32(edi)
   12 | STle(t3) = 0xcccccccc
   13 | t8 = Add32(t3,t6)
   14 | PUT(edi) = t8
   NEXT: PUT(eip) = 0x00000005; Ijk_Boring
```
But when trying to lift "\xb9\x04\x00\x00\x00\xb8\xcc\xcc\xcc\xcc\xf3\xab":
```
mov ecx, 4
mov eax, 0xcccccccc
rep stosd
```
we get:
```
   00 | ------ IMark(0x0, 5, 0) ------
   01 | PUT(ecx) = 0x00000004
   02 | ------ IMark(0x5, 5, 0) ------
   03 | PUT(eax) = 0xcccccccc
   04 | PUT(eip) = 0x0000000a
   05 | ------ IMark(0xa, 2, 0) ------
   06 | PUT(ecx) = 0x00000003
   07 | t7 = GET:I32(dflag)
   08 | t6 = Shl32(t7,0x02)
   09 | t3 = GET:I32(edi)
   10 | STle(t3) = 0xcccccccc
   11 | t8 = Add32(t3,t6)
   12 | PUT(edi) = t8
   NEXT: PUT(eip) = 0x0000000a; Ijk_Boring
```

The first translation seems to be the correct one.
Do you have any idea why the second translation works differently?

The code is lifted using:
```py
pyvex.IRSB(<code>, 0, archinfo.ArchX86())
```

issue_title: Spotters: Lifters that help other lifters when they fail (using Gymrat)
issue_body:
This is a tracking issue for the effort to implement "Spotters" -- lifters that fix-up or implement missing features in other lifters.

The primary goal of this in the short term is to add hot-patches to libVEX using the gymrat framework, as we expect this to be 100000x easier than modding VEX itself.
For example, let's say we want to implement `svc` in ARM Cortex M; I don't expect the VEX folks to ever want to do that.  So we'll do it! But modding libVEX is complex, and I don't want to write a full gymrat lifter for ARM.  A Spotter here would be perfect.
 
Some scratch ideas:
- Lifters in pyvex are registered in "order", and tried in order, and if they fail, keep trying them
- rhelmot says we should be able to get partial blocks from VEX. This is the crucial trick.

Tasks:
1. Merge `feature/expanded_reality` into master so gymrat actually works
2. Mod the primary lifter mechanism so that partial block info is available to future lifters
3. Create a `Spotter` class (in lifter_helper.py) appropriate for these sorts of lifters.
4. Success criteria: We can do Cortex M4 hard FPU and maybe SVC, etc instructions this way.

issue_title: Ganga fails to lock files on NFSv4 mount
issue_body:
Hello,

I am currently debugging an issue with a user, who tries to run Ganga v602r3 on our batch farm BIRD/NAF at DESY.
Ganga tries to store the output file on an NFSv4 exported GPFS filesystem, however it fails with the following error message:

```
ERROR Error: [Errno 9] Bad file descriptor
ERROR A severe error occurred in the Repository 'prep.metadata':

Unable to launch due to underlying filesystem not working with unix
locks.Please try launching again with
[Configuration]lockingStrategy=FIXED to start Ganga without multiple
session support.
ERROR If you believe the problem has been solved, type "reactivate()"
to re-enable
INFO Ganga is now attempting to shut down all running processes
accessing the repository in a clean manner
INFO ... Please be patient!
ERROR Unable to disable Internal services, they may have already been
disabled!
```

I have traced this error back to the [dry_run_unix_locks](https://github.com/ganga-devs/ganga/blob/6c4a7cfb75a138b14e7f8c364faee3c27413e116/python/Ganga/Core/GangaRepository/SessionLock.py#L258) function in SessionLock.py.
The problematic part seems to be the following line:

`with open(test_file) as f_lock:`

The file is opened read-only, however this behaves different on NFSv3 and NFSv4.
On NFSv3 exports, opening the file as RO is working, while on NFSv4 the Bad File Descriptor error message is logged.
According to a post on the [NFS mailing list](http://www.spinics.net/lists/linux-nfs/msg18594.html), this is an expected behavior.

If the lock file is opened with Read/Write, it works on NFSv4.
Please let me know, if you need more information.

Regards,
Stefan

issue_title: Avoid recursion
issue_body:
We are using recursion to "wait" for events to occur in two places:

* `wait_for_idle` on `LivySession`

* `_send_request_helper` on `ReliableHttpClient`

Since by default `wait_for_idle` waits 3600 seconds and sleeps for 2 seconds at a time, it is likely to reach the default recursion limit of 1000 calls.  

`_send_request_helper` is unlikely to hit the recursion limit because the retry limit is not very high, but it should still be avoided.

issue_title: Make IPython version requirement less restrictive
issue_body:
Right now we're pinning to IPython v4.0.2, which is inconvenient for users running other IPython versions.  4.0.2 isn't even the most recent IPython, [4.1.1 is](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/ipython/4.1.1).

We should make this a less strict inequality so that we can accept any version of IPython >= 4.0.2 (for example).

issue_title: Source of spikes from multicomp cell is ~random segment if none named 'soma'
issue_body:
[This code](https://github.com/Neurosim-lab/netpyne/blob/development/netpyne/cell.py#L543) in associateGid() for placing a NetConn which will be the source of spikes for that cell's gid:

```
 sec = self.secs['soma'] if 'soma' in self.secs else self.secs[self.secs.keys()[0]] # use soma if exists, otherwise 1st section
```

will try to select the 'first' section if there is none called 'soma'. However there is no guarantee that the key returned by self.secs.keys()[0] will be for the root section, it depends how the cell was created and how the list ordering is handled. 

It might be better to look through `self.secs` to find the section which has no parent (topol = {}) and use this:
```
 for s in self.secs:
     if len(self.secs[s]['topol'])==0: # i.e. no parent 
          sec = self.secs[s]
```

issue_title: `convert_column_to_enum` doesn't work at all
issue_body:
This issue is related with three packages: `graphql-core`, `graphene` and `graphene-sqlalchemy`. Actually there are 2 separated problems related with the Enum type:

1. In `convert_column_to_enum`, the returned value should be an instance of `UnmountedType` but not a subclass of `UnmountedType`.
2.[ `ChoiceType` of `sqlalchemy_utils`](https://github.com/kvesteri/sqlalchemy-utils/blob/0.32.9/sqlalchemy_utils/types/choice.py) coerces the result to `Choice` type or Enum subclass type (depend on which `XxxxTypeImpl` it used). But it's more pain in ass since there's no way to override [`GraphQLEnumType`'s `serialize` method](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/blob/v0.5.2/graphql/type/definition.py#L474) like `GraphQLScalarType` did.

issue_body:

issue_title: Confusing failure in remote mode for GAPIC config pipeline
issue_body:
### Expected behavior
If a GAPIC config already exists and the pipeline is running in remote mode, either
- artman fails and returns a meaningful error message, or
- artman overwrites the config in the archive that is downloaded

### Observed behavior
Artman fails with no information. Logs indicate only that the config was generated successfully, since the failure occurs at the copying stage.

### Context
Artman does not automatically overwrite existing configs because of the risk of overwriting uncommitted manual edits, which occur frequently in the development process. However, this risk exists only when running Artman in local mode, since in remote mode, nothing is ever overwritten on the local machine. We can therefore safely overwrite.

If detecting the environment (local/remote) is complicated, a clear error message is a good short-term solution.

I think 914-917 need to be removed. Looks like they were copied from `win_exists()`, but maybe left on accident. 

If the selected `win` does exist then the result is JSON + "true" appended on the end which makes the json invalid (json.loads() fails):

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20519655/39486683-a0d4d126-4d42-11e8-852b-564bd714bc89.png)

If the selected `win` does not exist then the assert fails so "false" never gets written. The returned result is an empty string with an exception logged on the server. 

I removed the lines locally and `json.loads(vis.get_window_data('compare'))` works like a champ and passing in a `win` that does not exist prints exception on server logs and returns an empty string. This seems like fine behavior if the `win` doesn't exists, but I'd leave that decision you guys. 

Related to PR: #272

issue_title: Crashes with "Encountered NaN"
issue_body:
I stumbled on a problem that seems to consistently crash fastText when using a particular training data set for classification and specific training options. This seems to be very sensitive to both the data set and the parameters: if I change anything then most likely the crash will go away.

I'm using fastText 97fcde80ea107ca52d3d778a083564619175039c (Dec 14, 2017) from the master branch. My OS is Ubuntu 16.04 amd64. `gcc --version` reports `gcc (Ubuntu 5.4.0-6ubuntu1~16.04.5) 5.4.0 20160609`.

Here is what happens:

```
$ ./fasttext supervised -input train200k.txt -output model2 -lr 1.0 -epoch 25 -loss hs
Read 2M words
Number of words:  360860
Number of labels: 25272
Progress:   12.4% words/s:   1441702 lr:  0.875922 loss: 118.363022 eta:   0h 0mterminate called after throwing an instance of 'std::runtime_error'
terminate called recursively
  what():  Encountered NaN.terminate called recursively
terminate called recursively
Aborted (core dumped)
```

Often the error message is a bit shorter than this but this was one of the more verbose runs. Also the crash happens at slightly different progress percentages between 12% and 13%.

I've put up the training data here (gzipped): http://tester-os-kktest.lib.helsinki.fi/fastText/train200k.txt.gz

This originally happened with a larger training data set but I was able to cut it down to these 200k lines. If I cut it further the crash will not happen anymore.

I tried on a different Ubuntu 16.04 machine too (same gcc version) and got the same crash so I believe this should be reproducible.

issue_title: npy_format_descriptor may not be accurate for platform-dependent lengths of `short`, `long`, and `long long`
issue_body:
Ran into this via an esoteric route: When tinkering with user-defined dtypes, it seems that implementation of `np.ones` uses `np.copyto(dst, 1, casting='unsafe')`, where the `1` gets interpreted as `np.array(1, dtype=np.int64)` (at least, on my platform).

When I tried registered a caster using `npy_descriptor_format<int64_t>::dtype()` using the `type_num` field, I realized its value was for `NPY_LONGLONG`, whereas `NPY_INT64` corresponds to `NPY_LONG`.

Will try to see if I can create a failing test, and then submit a tentative fix PR based on that.

issue_title: Inconsistent detection of python version in pybind11 and default cmake
issue_body:
If both python 2.7 and 3.5 are installed in Linux there is an inconsistent behavior.
cmake functions `find_package(PythonInterp)` and `find_package(PythonLibs)` find  2.7 by default.
In contrast pybind11 finds 3.5 by default.
This leads to weird bugs in complex projects where pybind11 is used along with other python-related things because the order of find_package in CMakeLists.txt matters:

```
find_package(PythonInterp)
find_package(PythonLibs)
find_package(pybind11)
message(STATUS "Found pybind11 v${pybind11_VERSION}: ${pybind11_INCLUDE_DIRS}")

-- Found PythonInterp: /usr/bin/python (found version "2.7.12") 
-- Found PythonLibs: /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libpython2.7.so (found version "2.7.11") 
-- Found PythonLibs: /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libpython2.7.so
-- Performing Test HAS_CPP14_FLAG
-- Performing Test HAS_CPP14_FLAG - Success
-- Found pybind11 v2.2.dev0: /home/semen/programs/pteros/include;/usr/include/python2.7
```

Now the opposite way:
```
find_package(pybind11)
message(STATUS "Found pybind11 v${pybind11_VERSION}: ${pybind11_INCLUDE_DIRS}")
find_package(PythonInterp)
find_package(PythonLibs)

-- Found PythonInterp: /usr/bin/python3.5 (found version "3.5.2") 
-- Found PythonLibs: /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libpython3.5m.so
-- Performing Test HAS_CPP14_FLAG
-- Performing Test HAS_CPP14_FLAG - Success
-- Found pybind11 v2.2.dev0: /home/semen/programs/pteros/include;/usr/include/python3.5m
-- Could NOT find PythonLibs (missing:  PYTHON_INCLUDE_DIRS)
```

This is very confusing. Imho, pybind11 should find 2.7 by default to be consistent with what one expects from default cmake behavior.
At least it worth mentioning this clearly in docs.

issue_title: pybind::buffer_info view double deletion
issue_body:
Perhaps I am not using the desired use strategy of buffer_info, but I'll assume I am for now.

I use buffer_info's to extract out the information from pybind::array types (for numpy).

I wrote some routines to extract (return) buffer_info's from a py object covering a few different cases.

I started getting some very odd crashes that took me some time to figure out due to their double free nature and scoping of automatic storage variables/destructors.

As it turns out the problem boils down to this:

`py::buffer_info a = ndarray.acquire(); //OK`
````
py::buffer_info a {};
a = ndarray.acquire(); //NOT ok
```

My solution was to add move constructors and move assignment operators to py::buffer_info and write it as so:
````
py::buffer_info a {};
a = std::move(ndarray.acquire()); //works, but does this minimize user error/what library users would normally enter?
```

Perhaps it is better to use std::unique_ptr or std::shared_ptr, however I can think of issues with all of these approaches.  unique_ptr will mean we can't simply copy the buffer_info... and well, it's just info, right? At least at face value.  With shared_ptr, we get into the muddled waters of doubly referenced counted (on the python side, and on the C++ side),  and the increased possibility that the user leaves dangling python objects (views) around.  Perhaps we should also subclass buffer_info's who come from memoryview's so they don't pay the penalty of unnecessary shared_ptr's.

All things considered, I believe shared_ptr may be the most behaved and least error-prone (for users) approach.

If you want the move ctor/assignment operator, I'll be happy to submit a PR.  I added some other things to perform as expected more often as well.

issue_title: Shell expansion not available for password.fetch command
issue_body:
I currently use a password.fetch command to get the password for a dav server. The config line I use looks like this:

`password.fetch = ["command", "gpg", "-d", "--use-agent", "/absolute/path/to/some/file]`

Now I would like to replace the absolute path by something like `~/relative/path/to/some/file`. This does now work, however, since the `~` gets fed literally to gpg, which doesn't expand it. If you ran this command through a shell, expansion of `~` (or `$HOME` or whatever) would be available.

vdirsyncer version used is 0.14.1 running with python 3.4.2 on Debian Linux.

Before you submit bug reports: https://vdirsyncer.pimutils.org/en/stable/contributing.html

issue_title: ServerName not drawn from apache conf.d
issue_body:
I start letsencrypt-auto (without args) and get and error:

"No names were found in your configuration files.
You should specify ServerNames in your config...."

however I do have a ServerName entry in a file called apache's conf.d/fqdn, which apache seems to pick up fine.

(This doesn't block continuing, but picking up these values as well would improve the automation of letsencrypt)

issue_title: Authentication fails because of incorrect file ownership
issue_body:
As we know ./letsencrypt-auto will create a hash file at for example;

> http://www.example.com/.well-known/acme-challenge/xxx

My SSL install fails because the xxx file is owned by root and Apache is unable to deliver the file. This results in a 404 error and the authentication fails. 

Does anyone know how I can give the file correct user/group permissions? I can't see any option for this in the --help output.

issue_title: Finalize Python 3 support
issue_body:
We should be Python 3 compatible now! Before the next release though, we should:

* Spend some time playing with it to see if we can find a corner case where it breaks. If so, it should be added to our integration tests.
* Make `tools/venv3.sh` install Certbot and it's official plugins
* Modify the relevant `setup.py` files listing support for Python 3.

issue_title: Implement a good OS fingerprinting function in python
issue_body: It's miserable that we'll need to do this, but over in #1811 we [identified](https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/pull/1811#issuecomment-164518894) that `le_util.get_os_info()`, which relies on Python's `platform` module, isn't actually recognising operating systems very well.  @joohoi suggested doing it based on the `letsencrypt-auto` [fingerprinting model](https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/blob/cb713a200b0a951f81018ed647e6002f09cb2ceb/letsencrypt-auto#L122).  We could also do it based on what we see in `/etc/apache2/` itself.

issue_title: Certbot rollback and temporary checkpoints
issue_body:
Trying to explain to a user how to revert temporary changes is awful. Technically, the answer is run the client long enough so that Certbot creates an instance of the plugin you were using when the program crashed. Best answer I've come up with is to run `certbot plugins` which is awkward.

Instead, I think `certbot rollback` should ignore the `--checkpoints` value if there is a temporary checkpoint and just rollback that, telling the user what it did.

issue_title: Nginx plugin should (often) create a redirect server block for a domain when one doesn't exist
issue_body:
## My operating system is (include version):

Ubuntu 16.04

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):

Dev instructions

## I ran this command and it produced this output:

Starting with the default Nginx config on Ubuntu 16.04, I ran:
```
$ sudo certbot -d example.org,www.example.org --nginx --redirect
...
No matching insecure server blocks listening on port 80 found.
No matching insecure server blocks listening on port 80 found.
...
```

## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because:
Nginx is listening on port 80 and continuing to serve insecure HTTP traffic for these domains.

Probably what we should do is if no matching server block is found is if Nginx is listening on port 80 at all, we should create new server block(s) for the purpose of redirecting the traffic.

cc @ohemorange

issue_title: acme: Change public interfaces to receive / give PEM instead of OpenSSL types
issue_body:
Per https://github.com/pyca/pyopenssl/issues/291, the `cryptography` package probably covers all the things we need from pyOpenSSL, meaning we could in theory drop the depedency. One particular stumbling block: the `acme` library has public interfaces that take OpenSSL, in particular `CertificateRequest`, `CertificateResource`, `Revocation`, `request_issuance`, `poll_and_request_issuance`, `fetch_chain`, and `revoke`.

We should probably take a major semver change to turn these all into giving/receiving PEM, so the `acme` library can be agnostic of whether people are using `pyOpenSSL`, `cryptography`, or something else.

issue_body:
We used this concept a lot when developing Certbot and it's often a useful abstraction for thinking about certificates. We've never properly explained this term, however, so we can't really use it in our documentation.

We may or may not want to explain how lineages work to the average user, but at the very least they should be documented somewhere.

The current implementation "dispatches" messages solely on the token address without considering the current registry, this means that channels from two different registries but same token will be mixed up.

- Either:

- [ ] add `args['rpc']` and `args['server_address'] = localhost:<FREE PORT>` in `raiden/ui/cli.py::smoketest()`
- [ ] pass `server_address` to `raiden/tests/utils/smoketest.py::run_smoketest`
- [ ] query the restful RPC endpoint in `run_smoketest`

issue_title: Eclipse recognizes every DICOM set generated by pytrip as the same patient
issue_body:
![matchingname](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/22157203/23229784/d77fc628-f941-11e6-8fb9-adab14037e9f.png)
Here Eclipse thinks that the pytrip-generated DICOM set of Alinas patient from #245  is the same as the test files sent by Leszek and Niels  in regard to #193 (which is clearly not the case).  
The patient ID1 is always 123456 for DICOM sets generated with pytrip. Therefore Eclipse thinks it is the same patient. This seems to happen no matter what the other UIDs say because clearly they are listed with different names ("tst002" vs "cube"), being the original names of the respective .hed file.

This issue makes it a bit difficult to joggle with more than one pytrip-generated DICOM set at a time, because you have to delete the existing patient first in order to import the new one.

issue_title: Fetch results from remote host (sos fetch -r HOST)
issue_body:
With the updated remote execution model, we can execute a workflow on remote host. Everything happens on remote hosts and it is users responsibility to copy input files over and retrieve results if needed.

Although it is not too difficult for users to synchronize files with command `rsync`, I am wondering if we can simplify this process with a subcommand. For example,

```
sos fetch result.txt -r HOST
```
would fetch `result.txt` from `HOST`, using the `path_map` that has been defined in `hosts.yml`.

If pushing files to remote host is also necessary, perhaps we can implement

```
sos push input_file -r HOST
sos pull output_file -r HOST
```

issue_title: SoS script check SoS version
issue_body:

issue_title: How to handle rename in sos workflows
issue_body:
Rename is a common operation in workflows but is surprisingly difficult to do in sos workflows. I mean, once a step has been executed and file name recorded, renaming the file would cause complex consequences (depending on signature mode etc).

Right now the only sensible way to handle rename seems to rename the files immediately after they are generated and use the new names as step output. We should perhaps figure out something easier.

issue_title: Special token index guarantee.
issue_body:
**Context**
Special tokens are frequently used for masking or padding or interpreting the model. It's important in a Encoder/Decoder context that the decoder and encoder share the same indexes for EOS, SOS, and PAD. 

**Problem**
Creating two fields, one for French and one for English, there are no class constants for the index of `eos_token`. The only way to find out the index of `eos_token` is per instance of the class (etc. `self.stoi[eos_token]`).

The code by default is not designed to guarantee that the French dictionary has the same EOS index as the English dictionary.

**Possible Solution A**
With setting the optional parameter 'eos_token' would it be possible to set 'eos_token_index'?

**Possible Solution B**
Vocab or Field constant for the index of special tokens.

issue_title: Protected ComponentResource does not prevent deletion of child resources
issue_body:
My naive expectation was that setting `protect` to `true` on a `ComponentResource` would prevent the deletion of the component and all it's children.

However, we process the deletions of the children prior to the deletion of the component, so we gladly delete them before we discover and report that we cannot delete the componet, at which point we stop (after deleting all the contents of the component).

It may be that this is by design, though it is suprising.

It is of course still possible to explicitly pass the `protect` value down into child resources to ensure they are also protected.

issue_title: What's the best way to control which GPUs a worker can use?
issue_body:
Right now, we allow tasks to specify that they require GPUs by including it in the decorator, e.g.,

```python
@ray.remote(num_gpus=2)
def f():
  ...
```

#302 introduces the same syntax for actors, e.g.,

```python
@ray.actor(num_gpus=3)
class Foo(object):
  ...
```

So how does the function `f` actually know which GPUs to use?

With CPUs, the OS will try to balance things between CPUs, so it's less critical to get it right. That said, the local scheduler (or whoever) could set the affinity of different worker processes for different CPUs to control which CPUs each worker can use. Is there an analogue of all this for GPUs?

GPUs seem different from CPUs. The burden of choosing *which* GPU to use is often placed on the programmer, not on the OS. For example, the standard way of controlling which GPUs TensorFlow uses is to set the environment variable `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES`, e.g., something like `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=0,3,4` before running `tf.Session()`. Once you create a session, TensorFlow will reserve a bunch of memory on all visible GPUs. I'm not sure how specific the concept of using GPU device IDs is to TensorFlow or if it is general.

We can expose a method `ray.get_gpu_ids()` that could be called inside any task or any actor and would return the IDs (e.g., `[0, 3, 4]`) of the GPUs that that process is allowed to use. This assumes that environment variables can be set from within Python. In the case of TensorFlow, that works, e.g., we can do `os.environ["CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES"] = ",".join([str(i) for i in ray.get_gpu_ids()])` or something like that from Python (e.g., within an actor constructor) **as long as we do it before we run `tf.Session()`**. But you could imagine a scenario or a different library where the environment variable has to be set BEFORE the worker process is created (or before the library is imported). In that case, there are other options (e.g., having the local scheduler set the environment variable), but they all seem awful (actually, I ran into such a situation recently where I had to set an environment variable like `DISPLAY=:99` before importing a library because otherwise the library crashed when looking for an X server and brought down the worker).

issue_title: Restricting Ray to particular NUMA zone?
issue_body:
Larger machines have 2 physical sockets (ie, p3.16xlarge) so memory is non-uniform.
Is there a way to restrict Ray to use a specific NUMA zone?

In PyTorch, this can be done by using `GOMP_CPU_AFFINITY` flag, and gives increased performance for addition in some cases --
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/5412#issuecomment-368693766

If PyTorch is restricted to use NUMA zone 0, it may improve performance when Ray results lie in the same numa zone.

issue_title: Feature request: Accept any M3U8 without needing to identify a plugin
issue_body:
----

### Checklist

- [ ] This is a bug report.
- [x] This is a feature request.
- [ ] This is a plugin (improvement) request.
- [ ] I have read the contribution guidelines.

### Description

Simply allow processing a plain M3U8 file the same as it's processed once a plugin finds it.

**Cons**

- Is not entirely 'on mission'
- May need a special cli flag, which would be non-intuitive
- May need some re-writing at a deeper level if the logic of everything goes through plugins

**Pros**

- Allows users to use streamlink no matter the source instead of first deciding whether it's a supported stream or an M3U8
- The flag could be as simple as `--bypass-plugins`, which opens the door to other possibilities in future

### Reproduction steps / Explicit stream URLs to test

1. `streamlink http://stream1.shopch.jp/HLS/out1/prog_index.m3u8`
`error: No plugin can handle URL: http://stream1.shopch.jp/HLS/out1/prog_index.m3u8`
2. `streamlink http://video3.earthcam.com:1935/fecnetwork/4717.flv/playlist.m3u8`

3. `streamlink http://200.75.19.171/uoct/13/playlist.m3u8`

issue_title: False-negative when function call follows ControlFlowNode
issue_body:
Just moved to SF! Figured I would talk to myself in a GitHub issue to reacquaint myself with what I was doing.

This issue was the only test to fail on https://github.com/python-security/pyt/pull/63, similar to the [-1] issue it only happens with function calls because it is the only place in the codebase where we "artificially" create nodes.

The reason why the test passed in master and not on the https://github.com/python-security/pyt/pull/63 PR was because in master only user-defined functions were turned into more than 1 node, and in the https://github.com/python-security/pyt/pull/63 PR blackbox calls are supported as well. (The test only has blackbox calls.)
(
Before it was something like
```python
  param = request.args.get('param', 'not set')
```

Now it is something like 
```python
  ¤call_1 = ret_request.args.get('param', 'not set')
  param = ¤call_1
```
)


So if master had tests like below but with say outer() being user-defined and replacing scrypt.outer below, the same problem occurs.

```python
import scrypt

image_name = request.args.get('image_name')
for x in range(0, 10):
  print(x)
# print('if this print statement is here everything works')
foo = scrypt.outer(image_name) # Any call after ControlFlowNode causes the problem
send_file(foo)
```

```python
import scrypt

image_name = request.args.get('image_name')
if not image_name:
    image_name = 'foo'
# print('if this print statement is here everything works')
foo = scrypt.outer(image_name) # Any call after ControlFlowNode causes the problem
send_file(foo)
```

I gave 2 examples here because it is not specific to `if` or `for`, but any ControlFlowNode followed by a function call.


**Where and why does this occur though?**

In stmt_star_handler in base_cfg.py we have a call to connect_nodes `self.connect_nodes(cfg_statements)`.
The purpose is to connect one node to the next node. (n->n+1 on and on.)

The problem is that `foo = ¤call_2` is connected to both the if statement and last statement of it's body, instead of the first node of the call.
In the first example, this is `¤call_1 = ret_request.args.get('param', 'not set')` rather than `param = ¤call_1`.

You might say, what's so bad? You already made a new node type BBnode, just set a first_statement attribute as e.g. `¤call_1 = ret_request.args.get('param', 'not set')` and you'd kind of be right.
Except nested function calls make that a pain, because I'll need to follow a chain of "What's your first node?" "What's your first node?".
e.g. if it was ``¤call_2 = ret_request.args.get(¤call_1, 'not set')` we would need first_statement to be the first_statement of ¤call_1.
I think this is the way forward, but I don't feel good about it.

issue_body:
ref: http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/user/advanced/#proxies

Add documentation how pykechain can be used behind a proxy.

It boils down to add environment variables `HTTP_PROXY` and `HTTPS_PROXY`.

```
$ export HTTP_PROXY="http://10.10.1.10:3128"
$ export HTTPS_PROXY="http://10.10.1.10:1080"

$ python
>>> import requests
>>> requests.get('http://example.org')
```

issue_title: Best approach to building trio ecosystem
issue_body:
My async experience has largely been with the aio-lib ecosystem  (particularly http, websockets, elasticsearch and kafka).  I would love to have this functionality with trio and would happily contribute to help make it happen.  A few thoughts/questions:
1) If I wanted to for example add http functionality (which I believe trio currently lacks) would it be best to focus on porting aiohttp or perhaps focus on h11 or hyper-h2 (which already seem to have some basic curio integration).
2) Are there any useful patterns to port synchronous libraries (eg the official Elasticsearch library) to trio?
3) Would it be worthwhile to add subprojects under python-trio for various popular APIs (similar to aiol-lib)?

issue_title: repository_raw_blob returns 404
issue_body:
I'm having trouble getting file contents through api v4 now, and I think it's a problem with the url that the library is hitting.

https://github.com/python-gitlab/python-gitlab/blob/master/gitlab/v4/objects.py#L1878

`url = "/projects/%s/repository/raw_blobs/%s" % (self.id, sha)`

This doesn't seem to match the [Gitlab API specifications](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/api/repositories.html#raw-blob-content):

>`GET /projects/:id/repository/blobs/:sha/raw`

Sure enough, when I try to hit the endpoint in postman only the latter route returns file contents, whereas `GET /projects/:id/repository/raw_blobs/:sha` consistently returns a 404.

issue_title: Better enrichment of Git and GitHub raw indices to calculate metrics for Manuscripts
issue_body:
Hey all
I've been trying to calculate the GMD metrics and to add them to Manuscripts.
There are some fields and values that I think should be added to the enriched index when it is being created so that it becomes easier to calculate the metrics related to those fields. The metrics can be derived from some other field in the enriched index, but never the less, I think there is a need to produce better enriched indices for Git and GitHub data sources, for now.
There are several Metrics which need additional data in the enriched indices, and they are as follows:

- [Issue Resolution Efficiency](https://github.com/chaoss/metrics/tree/master/activity-metrics/issue-resolution-efficiency.md). There is an [issue opened under WG-GMD](https://github.com/chaoss/wg-gmd/issues/5) and as you can see from the discussion, we actually need to figure out if issues have a proposed (and accepted PR) for them to be able to get the title of a successfully closed issue.

- [First Response to Issue Duration](https://github.com/chaoss/metrics/tree/master/activity-metrics/first-response-to-issue-duration.md). [Related issue in WG-GMD](https://github.com/chaoss/wg-gmd/issues/8). We may need to calculate the time to first response to the issue.

**NOTE:** I also wanted to put this out there, I think we need to interlink issues and PRs by mentioning the issue number in the proposed PR and vice versa. This way it'll be easier to track down both of them and see the progress.

- [Code Reviews](https://github.com/chaoss/metrics/tree/master/activity-metrics/code-reviews.md): as far as I can tell, there is no information about the reviews that are given to a PR. This information needs to be fetched using the API and added into the enriched index.

- [Code Merge Duration](https://github.com/chaoss/metrics/tree/master/activity-metrics/code-merge-duration.md) | What is the duration of time between code merge request and code commit?

- [Code Review Efficiency](https://github.com/chaoss/metrics/tree/master/activity-metrics/code-review-efficiency.md) | What is the number of merged code changes/number of abandoned code change requests?
- [Maintainer Response to Merge Request Duration](https://github.com/chaoss/metrics/tree/master/activity-metrics/maintainer-response-to-merge-request-duration.md) | What is the duration of time for a maintainer to make a first response to a code merge request?
- [Code Review Iteration](https://github.com/chaoss/metrics/tree/master/activity-metrics/code-review-iteration.md) | What is the number of iterations that occur before a merge request is accepted or declined?
- [Forks](https://github.com/chaoss/metrics/tree/master/activity-metrics/forks.md) | Forks are a concept in distributed version control systems like GitHub. It is a proxy for the approximate number of developers who have taken a shot at building and deploying the codebase *for development*.

- [Pull Request Comment Duration](https://github.com/chaoss/metrics/tree/master/activity-metrics/pull-requests-comment-duration.md) | The difference between the timestamp of the pull request creation date and the most recent comment on the pull request.
- [Pull Request Comment Diversity](https://github.com/chaoss/metrics/tree/master/activity-metrics/pull-requests-comment-diversity.md) | Number of each people discussing each pull request.
- [Pull Request Comments](https://github.com/chaoss/metrics/tree/master/activity-metrics/pull-request-comments.md) | Number of comments on each pull request. 


I think these or fields from which these can be derived need to be added to the enriched indices.

I am also adding issues for the GMD Metrics which need more polishing and definition  under [WG-GMD](https://github.com/chaoss/wg-gmd/issues) repository.
I plan on generating PRs to add the above metrics and this issue can be used as the Parent issue for the same.

issue_title: Blurr Playground: A jupyter notebook with a full example featuring BTS and Sample Data
issue_body:
#### Issue Checklist:
- [ ] Descriptive title
- [ ] Completed sections below
- [ ] Bug report? Relevant debug logs / reproduction information

### Problem Description
Our examples are static and showcase how different problems can be solved using Blurr.  However, when one wants to try out Blurr hands-on they need to go though the full project setup before they can start tinkering with the Blurr Transform Spec.  The friction in the process adds a huge impedance to adoption.

### Solution Proposal
Examples should be provided in the form of a Jupyter Notebook.  A notebook allows the context of the problem being solved, the solution details, etc. to be specified in markdown blocks.  The user can simply execute blocks to load the BTS and transform the sample data included.

Expected behavior:
1. Launch Notebook
2. Read through the descriptors
3. Walks though the BTS yaml
4. Validate BTS
5. Run BTS on sample data
6. Observe and compare results with documentation block
7. Follow instructions to make changes to BTS
8. Run modified BTS on sample data
9. Verify results

The Notebook should provide the following:
1. Full environment with the latest Blurr package, Sample Data and BTS.
2. Must be a fresh environment at launch - changes made by each user must be limited to their own kernel

### Impact on current behavior
This is an extension to what we already have.  All changes are expected to improve usability.

issue_title: Allow users to specify a filter function to remove endpoints from auto doc
issue_body:
I'm using httdomain on a tornado project with "auto tornado" to generate API documentation and it's great. A nice-to-have feature is the ability to specify a custom filter function to the autotornado directive to allow for greater dynamic control over which API routes are published vs. not. A few use cases include:

* Private/internal APIs endpoints that shouldn't be accessed by end-users
* "Unstable" APIs that are intended for eventual public consumption but should remain undocumented in official docs until the API has stabilized

I'd propose a "filter-endpoints" parameter to the auto tornado directive. It would take a tornado rule target (Rule or URLSpec) or a method/function target of such rule and return True to exclude the item (the item is filtered) and False to include it (the item is not filtered).

For instance, a filter function that excludes classes and methods which have "PRIVATE API" in the docstring:

```
def doctoring_private_filter(obj):
    return "PRIVATE API" in obj.__doc__
```

And would be setup as:
```
    .. autotornado:: yourwebapp:app
        :filter-endpoints: yourwebapp:docstring_private_filter
```

issue_title: Parser throws error if text to parse doesn't end with a new line.
issue_body:
This was a problem I came across in a grammar I was writing, but I was able to reproduce it with some of the examples. I don't know if this is Lark working as designed and just grammars are defined incorrectly, or if this is a problem with Lark.

If you start a grammar with a line like: `start: (_item | _NL)*` and then try to read a file that doesn't end with a new line, it will throw a bad parse error. Reproduce by using [lark.lark](https://github.com/lark-parser/lark/blob/master/examples/lark.g) as the grammar and then try to parse it without a `\n` at the end of the file.

The easy solution to this is, of course, just add a newline at the end of any string you want to parse. But this doesn't feel like a very good solution. Any thoughts of how to fix?
